Certain IP addresses can be assigned freely on the local area network. Since they are not unique, they are not routed on the internet. The addresses which can be freely assigned are called private IP addresses, and the ones which are unique are called public. To be routed, a private address needs to be translated into a public one. This process of translating between private and public addresses is called network address translation, or NAT. Routers and firewalls often also perform this task. Might be kind of fun to chew on. Reading Comprehension Introduction: Ask your student to remember walks he has taken. Ask: Where did you go? What did you see? Who went with you? Then introduce the book Jonathan and His Mommy by Irene Smalls. Read aloud the title and the names of the author and illustrator. Tell your student that this book is about a walk, too. Explain that good readers think about the way a book makes them feel. Say: As I read, I will think about the way the story makes me feel. When I look at the cover, it looks like Jonathan and his mother are people who like to have fun. I like to have fun, too. As I read, I will ask myself questions about the story to see if I like it and why. Instruction: Explain to your student that good readers pay attention to important story parts to help them understand a story better. Say: One important story part is the characters ; they are who the story is about. In this story, Jonathan and his mother are the main characters. Page through the book, looking at the pictures. Explain that another important story part is the setting , which is where the story takes place. Say: The pictures tell me that Jonathan and his mother live in a city neighborhood. I see apartment buildings, sidewalks, streets, stores, a basketball court, and a park. Read the story with expression, changing your voice as Jonathan and his mother change how they move. |Tip: Click on the link below to watch a sample BrainPOP Jr. movie. | Help your student summarize the story by asking the following questions. Who are the main characters in the story? (Jonathan, mother) Where does the story take place? (city neighborhood) What did Jonathan and his mother do in the story? (walk and talk) What kinds of steps did they take? (zigzag, giant, itsy-bitsy baby, bunny, fast, running, slow-motion, ballet, crisscross, reggae, backward, Jonathanand- Mommy) Why do you think Jonathan likes to go walking and talking with his mother? Would you like to walk and talk like this? Why or why not? What was your favorite part of the story? How did this book make you feel? Assist your student in completing pp. 99-100 of the Practice Book . Phonemic Awareness Introduction: Read the following clues to introduce Alphafriend Tiggy Tiger. Say: Our new Alphafriend's sound is /t/. Say it with me: /t/. This animal is a kind of wild cat that lives in the jungle. This cat purrs, but it also growls-so watch out! He has orange and black stripes on his fur and sharp claws. Ask: What animal is our new Alphafriend? (tiger) His name is Tiggy Tiger. Show the student the Alphafriend Card Tiggy Tiger. Instruction: Read or sing "Tiggy Tiger's Song" to your student. Note: Listen to Track 5 on The Music Break: Disc One for the tune. Tiggy Tiger's Song (tune: "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star") Tiggy Tiger can tickle his toes. Tiggy Tiger can tap his nose. Tiggy Tiger can turn around. Tiggy Tiger can touch the ground. Tiggy Tiger can tie his shoes. Tiggy Tiger can count by twos. Note: The term onset refers to the sound of the letter or letters before the first vowel in a one-syllable word. Rime is the sound of the remaining part of the word. Say: I will say some sounds. You put them together to make words from the song: /c/ /an/ (can); /t/ /ap/ (tap). Continue with other one-syllable words from the song: toes, nose, turn, touch , and tie. Tell your student that he is going to take apart the words he just put together. Say: You blended the word toes . Say it with me: toes . Ask: What is the beginning sound? (/t/) What is the rest of the word? (/o-z/) Say the word with me: /t/ /o-z/ Repeat with other words from the song: nose, turn, touch , and tie Assist your student in completing pp. 101-102 of the Practice Book . Create a web to list the ways Jonathan and his mother moved. Application: Have your student practice using movement words. The brain needs a constant supply of blood to work. During a stroke, blood stops flowing to part of the brain. The affected area is damaged. Its functions are harmed or even lost. Most strokes are caused by a blockage in a blood vessel that supplies the brain. They can also occur if a blood vessel in the brain ruptures (breaks open). The heart is a pump. It sends oxygen-rich blood out through blood vessels called arteries. If an artery between the heart and the brain is blocked, the brain can't get enough oxygen. Some artery blockages are caused by plaque (fatty deposits). Arteries can also be blocked by blood clots. Some clots form on the plaque. Others can form in the heart-especially in people with atrial fibrillation (irregular heart rhythm). If a piece of plaque or clot breaks off and enters the bloodstream, it can flow to the brain and cause a stroke. Ischemic stroke occurs when an artery that supplies the brain is greatly narrowed or blocked. This can be caused by a buildup of plaque. It can also occur when small pieces of plaque or blood clot (called emboli) break off into the bloodstream. The emboli flow in the blood until they get stuck in a small blood vessel in the brain. Healthy Arteries: In a healthy artery, the lining of the artery wall is smooth. This lets blood flow freely from the heart to the rest of the body. The brain gets all the blood it needs to function well. Damaged Arteries: High blood pressure or other problems can roughen artery walls. This allows plaque to build up in the walls. Blood clots may also form on the plaque. This can narrow the artery and limit blood flow. [in the above seven lines mom is speaking in a puppet voice.] Those are m and m's in there, huh? Strictly speaking, the observable Universe depends on the location of the observer. By travelling, an observer can come into contact with a greater region of space-time than an observer who remains still, so that the observable Universe for the former is larger than for the latter. Nevertheless, even the most rapid traveler may not be able to interact with all of space. Typically, the 'observable Universe' means the Universe seen from our vantage point in the Milky Way Galaxy. Title: Adding or Changing Your Avatar on Howrse Introduction: Howrse is an online horse breeding game where players can raise, train, and compete with virtual horses. One way to personalize your experience on Howrse is by adding or changing your avatar. This tutorial will guide you through the process of doing so. Step 1: Log into Your Account Visit <https://www.howrse.com/> and enter your login credentials (username and password) to access your account. It's essential that you are logged in before attempting to change your avatar. Step 2: Navigate to Profile Settings Once logged in, hover over the 'My Account' tab located at the top right corner of the page. From the dropdown menu, select 'Settings'. This action takes you to the settings section where various customization options are available. Step 3: Access Appearance Options In the left-hand sidebar within the settings page, click on 'Appearance'. A new set of tabs will appear; choose the first one labeled 'Avatar'. You have now reached the destination for altering your profile picture. Step 4: Upload New Avatar Image Click on the 'Choose File' button under the text that reads "Upload your own image". Select the image file from your device that you wish to use as your avatar. Remember, the ideal size for an avatar photo is 100x100 pixels. If your chosen image does not meet these dimensions, consider resizing it using any basic photo editing software like Microsoft Paint or Preview (Mac). After selecting the file, press 'Open'. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Make sure the selected image represents yourself positively since it forms part of your public identity on Howrse. - Refrain from uploading copyrighted materials without proper permission. - Respect community standards regarding appropriateness when choosing your avatar. Step 5: Save Changes After successfully uploading your preferred image, do not forget to save changes made. Simply hit the blue 'Save' button found near the bottom of the page. The system might take a few seconds to load, but once completed, a confirmation message should pop up saying, "Your modifications were saved." What Happens Next? Upon saving the changes, your newly added or changed avatar becomes visible immediately across all pages of the website whenever you interact. Other users can see this too, enhancing the overall user engagement. Conclusion: Customizing your avatar adds a touch of personality to your Howrse gaming experience while fostering interaction among fellow gamers. By following our simple yet thorough instructional steps, modifying your avatar should be quick and effortless! So she'll look at them for a little bit, but she really likes this one. A Beginning Reading Lesson Rationale: This lesson teaches children about the short vowel correspondence a=/a/. As a beginning reader, it is important for students to learn the correspondences between letters and sounds. In this lesson children will learn to distinguish, spell, and read words containing the letter a. The students will also learn a meaningful illustration (baby crying saying Aaaa!); they will use this memorable connection to the correspondence in written and spoken language. The students will also use this correspondence in a Letterbox lesson, and read a decodable book that focuses on the correspondence a= /a/. Graphic image of baby crying Individual letterboxes (Elkonin Boxes) for the students Individual picture pages: (cat, bag, jam, man, hat, crab, hand) with the correct number of letterboxes underneath the picture Individual letter tiles for the students (a, m, c, t, s, d, h, g, r, b, l, n, k, p, s, r) Teacher letterboxes and letter tiles Individual copies of A Cat Nap for the students and teacher Primary paper and pencil for student 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining that letters represent different sounds in written words. In order to become excellent readers, we must learn how to match the letter to their sounds. When I say /a/ I think of a baby crying for ice cream this will be the perfect time to show the graphic image of the baby crying. Explain to the students that once they begin to understand the /a/ sound, they will be able to read and spell all kinds of words with short /a/. 2. Ask the students: Have you ever heard a babying crying aloud? What does the baby crying and screaming sound like when you hear it? When babies cry they scream and say Aaaaaa! The sound of a baby crying makes the short /a/ sound. It says Aaaaaa and we are going to remember this by making a crying noise whenever we see the letter /a/. [Have children act like they're crying like a baby to make the /a/ sound. 3. Have students find letter a in some words in this silly tongue twister."Alice asked Adam for an apple". Everybody say it aloud together. Now say it again, and this time, stretch the a at the beginning of each word that starts with a and think about the sound you make when you open your mouth wide and say"Aaa". 4. Now give each student a set of letterboxes as well as the appropriate letter tiles. Tell the students that they are going to be working with letterboxes and letter tiles to spell out some words with short a. "Now we are going to practice using our knowledge of the /a/ sound to spell words. What if I wanted to spell the word cat? I am going to start with the first sound that I hear in cat. Ccccat, I hear the /k/ sound. I will place the letter c in the first letterbox. The next sound I hear is Caaaat, /a/ there is the crying baby /a/ sound. Now I will place the phoneme a in my second letterbox. Now the last sound I hear is Cattt, /t/. I hear the /t/ sound, which means I will place the letter t in the last letterbox. Now I have made the word cat using letter tiles. 5. Now I want you to try to spell some words using your letterboxes and letter tiles and use the same method that I just modeled. The teacher should also have a word list ready for the students to spell that have the phonemes /a/ in it. As, the teacher call out a word, the students will put the letters in their boxes that they have in front of them. This way they will know how many boxes to have ready. Start out easy with two boxes for "at". "Look at that cat." What should go in the first box? (Respond to the children's answers). What goes in the second box? Check the student's progress while walking around the room. (Observe progress) You'll need three letterboxes for the next word. Listen for the beginning sounds to spell in the first box. Now listen for the /a/ sound in bat. "Look at the bat in the sky." (Allow students to spell remaining words: cat, jam, van, mat, pan, flat, and clap.) 6. Say: Now I am going to let you read the words you've spelled. Have the students read words in harmony. Afterwards call on individuals to read one word on the list until everybody has had a turn. 7. Say: Now we are going to read a book called A Cat Nap. (Hand out a copy of "A Cat Nap" to each student). Give a short book talk. This story is about a cat named Tab. Tab is a fat cat who likes to nap in a bag. Sam is the man who owns Tab. Sam plays baseball. Sam has a bat in his bag. To find out where Tab is while Sam plays baseball, you need to read. Let's pair up and take turns reading A Cap Nap to find out where Tab is. Students pair up and take turns reading alternate pages each while teacher walks around the room monitoring progress. 8. Say: Before we finish up with our lesson about the short /a/ sound, I want to complete a worksheet with some missing words so you solve a reading problem. Your job is to look in the box of word choices, and decide what word fits the pictures. First try reading all of the words in the box, then choose the word that fits best in the space. Reread your answers to see if they make sense. (Collect worksheets to evaluate individual child progress.) Sullivan, Sarah "The Scary Letter .aaaaaaaaa! Cushman, Sheila A Cat Nap. Galileo's experiments helped to create classical physics. Although he did not invent the telescope, he used it when he looked into the night sky. He supported Copernicus' idea that the Earth moved around the Sun (heliocentrism). He also investigated gravity. Isaac Newton used Galileo's ideas to create his three laws of motion and his law of universal gravitation. Together these laws explained the motion of falling bodies near the earth and the motion of earth and planets around the sun. Title: How to Change Location Settings in Windows 8 Windows 8 includes various location-based features that can be tailored to your preferences by adjusting the location settings. This tutorial will guide you through changing these settings step-by-step. **Step 1: Access the Charms Bar** The first step is to access the Charms bar, which provides quick access to several important functions such as Search, Share, Start, Devices, and Settings. To do this, move your cursor to the top right or bottom right corner of the screen until the Charms bar appears. Alternatively, press `Win + C` keys simultaneously on your keyboard. Key Tips & Guidelines: * If you're using a touchscreen device, swipe from the right edge towards the center of the display instead. * The Charms bar may take a moment to appear after moving your mouse; patience is required here. **Step 2: Open Settings** Once the Charms bar has appeared, click on 'Settings'. It should be located at the far right end of the charms menu. Clicking on 'Settings' will open up another panel with options related to PC settings, power, network, volume, etc. **Step 3: Choose Change PC Settings** In the new panel that opened up when you clicked 'Settings', look for an option labeled 'Change PC settings'. Clicking on this will bring you to the main settings page where you can modify different aspects of your system including privacy, users, wireless networks, ease of access, etc. **Step 4: Navigate to Privacy Section** From the list of categories displayed on the left side of the window under 'PC settings', select 'Privacy'. Here, you'll find numerous configuration options concerning your computer's data collection practices, app permissions, and advertising settings among others. **Step 5: Select Location Services** Under the heading 'Location' within the 'Privacy' section, locate the toggle switch for 'Location service'. Turning this ON allows apps and services to use information about your location. When turned off, no applications or services can determine your location. **Step 6: Manage Individual App Permissions** Directly beneath the master control for Location Service, there is a list of all installed applications along with their current permission status regarding location sharing. You can enable or disable location sharing individually for each application by toggling its corresponding button. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Disabling location services altogether might interfere with certain functionalities provided by some modern apps like weather forecasting or map navigation. However, disabling them individually ensures only necessary apps have access to your location info while maintaining overall security. **Step 7: Verify Default Sensor Settings** Still within the same 'Location' tab, scroll down further till you see 'Default sensor settings'. Make sure the correct default sensors are selected based on what hardware devices you have connected (e.g., GPS receiver). These sensors provide input used to calculate your machine's geographical position. And that concludes our comprehensive guide on changing location settings in Windows 8! By following these steps meticulously, you now have full command over who gets to know your precise coordinates, thus enhancing both functionality and privacy. He gave her some doll house furniture. Title: Get Your Friend to Stop Hitting On Your Boyfriend or Crush Introduction: It can be an uncomfortable situation when your friend starts showing interest in someone you have feelings for or are already dating. This guide will help you navigate this tricky social scenario with empathy, respect, and assertiveness. Remember that clear communication is essential; address the issue directly but tactfully while preserving both relationships. Here's how to handle the situation effectively: Step 1: Assess the Situation Objectively Analyze if there is genuine romantic interest from your friend towards your boyfriend or crush. Consider their actions, words, body language, and consistency over time. Understanding the severity and nature of the situation allows you to respond appropriately. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Avoid jumping to conclusions based solely on isolated incidents. - Evaluate whether the behavior makes your partner or crush feel uncomfortable as well. - Recognize any cultural differences that may influence perceptions and expectations around friendship boundaries. Step 2: Reflect on Personal Feelings Examine your emotions regarding the matter. Acknowledge potential jealousy, hurt, anger, or confusion without judging yourself harshly. Being honest about these feelings enables better understanding and decision-making. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Practice self-compassion during introspection. - Identify specific triggers that evoke strong emotional reactions. - Seek support from other trusted friends, family members, or mental health professionals if needed. Step 3: Establish Clear Boundaries Clearly define what behaviors make you uncomfortable and why they cross personal boundaries. Articulating these limits sets expectations for future interactions between all parties involved. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use "I" statements instead of blaming language (e.g., "I feel disrespected when..."). - Make sure to consider the perspectives and needs of everyone involved. - Revisit and adjust boundaries as necessary to maintain harmony within relationships. Step 4: Communicate Directly yet Tactfully Initiate a conversation with your friend expressing your concerns using assertive communication techniques. Express gratitude for their trust and openness while setting new parameters for interaction. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Schedule a private meeting at a neutral location. - Maintain composure throughout the discussion by practicing deep breathing exercises beforehand. - Listen actively and acknowledge their perspective before sharing yours. Example Script: "Hey [Friend], I appreciate our friendship and value your trust in me. However, I've noticed some behaviors recently that have made me uncomfortable due to my feelings for [Boyfriend/Crush]. Can we discuss ways to preserve our bond while also being mindful of those sentiments?" Step 5: Offer Alternatives and Solutions Propose alternative arrangements that allow continued camaraderie without compromising your relationship with your boyfriend or crush. Suggestions might include spending less one-on-one time together or engaging in group activities involving mutual acquaintances. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Focus on viable options rather than dwelling on problems. - Encourage input from your friend to ensure fairness and collaboration. - Be prepared to compromise and find middle ground acceptable to all parties. Step 6: Monitor Progress and Adjust Accordingly Regularly assess how changes impact relationships among the individuals involved. If further modifications become necessary, revisit conversations and revise agreements accordingly. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep dialogue ongoing to prevent miscommunication or misunderstandings. - Remind yourself and others of established boundaries as needed. - Show appreciation for cooperation and effort invested in maintaining healthy connections. Some dragonflies adjust their resting position to prevent overheating. They may use a handstand-like position to prevent overheating on sunny days. The abdomen is raised until its tip points at the sun, minimizing their surface area exposed to the heat. The position is called the obelisk posture. The species that do this are called 'perchers'; they are "sit and wait" predators which spend much of their time keeping still. Title: How to Behave When Liking a Best Friend's Boyfriend Introduction: When you find yourself having feelings for your best friend's boyfriend, it can be an incredibly challenging situation to navigate. It is essential to prioritize your friendship while handling this delicate matter with care and respect. This wikiHow will guide you through the steps to manage your emotions and maintain a healthy relationship with your best friend. Step 1: Acknowledge Your Feelings Recognize and accept that you have romantic feelings towards your best friend's boyfriend. Understanding your emotions allows you to make conscious decisions about how to proceed without denying or suppressing them. Key Tip: Keep your feelings private; do not share them with your best friend or her boyfriend at this stage. Disclosing your feelings may cause unnecessary distress and potentially harm your relationships. Step 2: Evaluate Your Emotions Assess whether your feelings are fleeting or genuine. If they are fleeting, give them time to pass and focus on maintaining your friendship. If they are genuine, consider if pursuing these feelings is worth risking your friendship. Guideline: Reflect on the strength and history of your friendship. Consider how much your friend means to you and whether losing her as a friend would be more painful than not being with her boyfriend. Step 3: Maintain Boundaries Establish clear boundaries between yourself and your best friend's boyfriend. Avoid spending one-on-one time together unless necessary, and ensure any interactions remain platonic and respectful. Key Tip: Communicate openly with both your best friend and her boyfriend about your intentions and the importance of preserving your friendship. Honesty builds trust and shows maturity. Step 4: Support Their Relationship Actively support your best friend and her boyfriend as a couple. Attend events together, engage in group activities, and express enthusiasm for their relationship. By fostering a positive environment, you demonstrate your commitment to your friend and help alleviate any potential tension. Guideline: Encourage open communication within their relationship by being available to talk and offer advice when asked. However, avoid interfering or offering unsolicited opinions. Step 5: Manage Jealousy It is natural to feel jealous from time to time. To cope with these feelings, practice gratitude for what you have in life, engage in self-care activities, and seek support from other friends or a therapist. Key Tip: Remember that everyone has unique experiences, and just because your friend is dating someone you like does not mean you cannot find happiness elsewhere. Focus on personal growth and self-improvement. Step 6: Address Conflicts Fairly If conflicts arise due to your feelings, approach them calmly and constructively. Clearly communicate your perspective, listen actively to the other person's viewpoint, and collaborate on finding a solution that benefits all parties involved. Guideline: Before addressing conflicts, take some time to reflect on your actions and determine where you might have gone wrong. Apologize sincerely if needed and work towards rebuilding trust. Conclusion: Navigating feelings for a best friend's boyfriend requires patience, empathy, and self-awareness. Prioritizing your friendship and managing your emotions responsibly ensures a healthier outcome for all involved. Don't even look at me if you're mad at me. Adding your own application to the new Yahoo Toolbar in Firefox can be an exciting way to enhance your web browsing experience. By following these steps closely, you'll learn how to add your custom application and understand why each action is important. **Step 1: Create or choose your application** Before adding an application to the Yahoo Toolbar, make sure that you have developed one using appropriate programming languages such as HTML, CSS, JavaScript, etc., or select from existing applications compatible with the toolbar. This will enable seamless integration between your chosen app and the Yahoo Toolbar. **Step 2: Package your application** Package your application into a .xpi file format which stands for Cross-Platform Installer package. To do this, create a directory named after your application containing all necessary files including index.html, script files, stylesheets, images, etc. Then, include a install.rdf file describing basic information like title, ID, version, and target application. Lastly, compress the entire folder into a single .xpi file. Key tip: Make sure to test your packaged application before proceeding further to ensure there are no bugs or errors affecting functionality. **Step 3: Access the Yahoo Toolbar configuration page** Open Firefox browser and navigate to about:config in the address bar. Accept any warning messages related to changing advanced settings. Next, search for general.useragent.extra.ytoolbar\_app\_id preference. If it doesn't exist, right click anywhere on the page, go to New > String, name it 'general.useragent.extra.ytoolbar_app_id', and leave its value empty for now. Guideline: Be cautious while modifying these settings; incorrect changes may negatively impact your browser performance. **Step 4: Register your application with the Yahoo Toolbar** Create a temporary bookmark by clicking the star icon at the end of the URL bar. Edit the newly created bookmark by giving it a meaningful name ("My App" for example) and pasting chrome://ytactionbuttons/content/actionButtonOverlay.js?appID={yourAppId} as its location. Replace {yourAppId} with a unique identifier string specific to your application. Save the bookmark afterwards. Key tip: Choose a descriptive name and a memorable yet distinct appID since they contribute to user understanding and future references. **Step 5: Enable Developer Mode** In your Firefox browser, type Ctrl+Shift+A (Windows/Linux) or Command + Option + A (Mac). The Add-ons Manager window should appear. Go to the gear icon located at the top-right corner and check "Load Temporary Add-on". Now, locate and load your previously created .xpi file. Guideline: Always double-check if your installed XPI appears under Extensions within the Add-ons Manager Window. **Step 6: Verify installation** Once loaded successfully, restart your Firefox browser. You should notice your added application visible either near other default buttons or grouped together based on their function. Click on it to confirm whether it works according to your expectations. Remember: Sometimes, due to compatibility issues or technical glitches, certain features might malfunction. Therefore, always maintain backups of original configurations to avoid permanent damage. By completing these steps, you've learned how to integrate your custom application onto the new Yahoo Toolbar in Firefox! Enjoy enriching your web browsing experience with your personalized tools. Title: How to Cut Meet for Jerky Introduction: Jerky is a popular snack that has been enjoyed for centuries. It's made by cutting meat into thin strips, seasoning it with spices, and then drying it. One of the most crucial steps in making jerky at home is properly cutting the meat. This guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation of how to cut meet for jerky, ensuring your final product is delicious and safe to eat. Things You'll Need: * A sharp knife (preferably a boning or slicing knife) * A cutting board * Fresh, lean meat (such as beef, venison, or turkey) * Paper towels Step 1: Choosing Your Meat Select a fresh, lean cut of meat from your local grocery store or butcher. Lean meats like flank steak, London broil, eye of round, or top round are ideal choices due to their low fat content. Fat can turn rancid during the drying process, affecting both taste and safety. Key Tip: Avoid using ground meat or mechanically tenderized meat, which have higher bacterial counts than solid muscle cuts. Step 2: Trimming Excess Fat Trim off any excess fat from the surface of the meat. Use your knife to carefully remove large pieces of fat, leaving only a small amount behind for flavor. Pat the trimmed meat dry with paper towels to ensure optimal moisture removal during the drying phase. Key Guideline: The more uniformly sized your meat slices are, the more evenly they will dehydrate, resulting in better texture consistency throughout your finished jerky. Step 3: Freezing the Meat Place the trimmed meat in the freezer for about 30 minutes to an hour before slicing. Partially frozen meat is easier to slice thinly and consistently compared to room temperature meat. Be careful not to freeze the meat completely; this could cause uneven slicing and inconsistent drying times. Step 4: Slicing Against the Grain Remove the partially frozen meat from the freezer and place it onto a clean cutting board. Locate the direction of the grain (muscle fibers) running through the meat. Slice perpendicular to these fibers, also known as cutting against the grain. Thin slices should be approximately - inch thick. Cutting against the grain ensures a tender chew when eating your homemade jerky. Key Tip: To maintain consistent thickness while slicing, try using a slicing guide or resting one end of the meat on a tall item such as a food can. Step 5: Final Preparation Once all the meat has been sliced, pat down any remaining moisture with paper towels. At this point, you may proceed with marinating the meat if desired. If choosing not to marinate, lay out the slices on dehydrator trays or oven racks lined with parchment paper. Proceed to the next step - dehydrating or slow cooking depending on your chosen method. By following these detailed instructions, you now possess the knowledge required to successfully cut meat for jerky. Remember to always prioritize food safety measures by selecting quality, lean meat and maintaining proper hygiene practices throughout the entire preparation process. Happy jerky making! Title: How to Make Perfect Cinnamon Toast Introduction: Cinnamon toast is a simple yet delicious treat that can be enjoyed at any time of day. It's an easy way to add some variety to your breakfast routine or satisfy your sweet tooth as a snack. This tutorial will guide you through the process of making perfect cinnamon toast, providing in-depth explanations for each step and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Gather Your Ingredients To make one serving of cinnamon toast, you'll need: - 2 slices of bread (preferably white or brioche) - 2 tablespoons of unsalted butter, softened - 2 teaspoons of granulated sugar - teaspoon ground cinnamon - A pinch of salt Key Tip: Using high-quality ingredients makes all the difference in this recipe. Opt for freshly ground cinnamon and good-quality bread for the best flavor. Step 2: Preheat the Oven Preheat your oven to 350 F (175 C). While the oven preheats, continue with the following steps. Guideline: Preheating the oven ensures that the toast becomes crispy without burning. The temperature specified provides enough heat to gently melt the butter and sugar while lightly toasting the bread. Step 3: Mix the Topping In a small bowl, combine the softened butter, sugar, cinnamon, and salt. Stir until well mixed. Key Tip: Ensuring the butter is soft allows for easier mixing and more even distribution of the cinnamon sugar over the bread. Step 4: Prepare the Bread Place the bread slices onto a baking sheet lined with parchment paper or a silicone mat. Spread half of the cinnamon sugar mixture onto each slice of bread, covering them evenly. Guideline: Be generous when spreading the mixture but avoid letting it pile up too much around the edges, which could cause uneven cooking or burning. Step 5: Bake the Toast Bake the prepared toast in the preheated oven for about 8-10 minutes, or until the edges are golden brown and the sugar has caramelized slightly. Key Tip: Keep an eye on the toast during the last few minutes of baking to prevent it from burning. Every oven is different, so adjust accordingly based on your own appliance's performance. Step 6: Cool and Serve Remove the cinnamon toast from the oven and allow it to cool for a minute before transferring it to a plate. Enjoy warm! Guideline: Allowing the toast to rest briefly keeps it from being too hot to handle and lets the flavors meld together. If desired, serve alongside milk, coffee, or tea for added enjoyment. Conclusion: Making perfect cinnamon toast requires just a handful of ingredients and minimal effort. By carefully following these steps and using quality components, you're sure to create a tasty treat that delights both kids and adults alike. Happy baking! [the school is going to a Christmas tree farm this morning.] Latin has seven different noun cases: nominative, vocative, accusative, genitive, dative, ablative and locative. The vocative case is almost always the same as the nominative case; however, if the nominative ends in -us, it changes to -e, and if the nominative ends in -ius, it changes to -i. The locative takes the form of the dative. Latin nouns are "declined", or changed, according to how they are used in the sentence. A noun can be declined five different ways. These ways are called declensions. The declensions are numbered 1 through 5 (first declension, second declension etc), each having different endings that identify the noun's declension. When a noun is declined, twelve forms are made, two for each of the noun cases (the locative is omitted). Jimmy and Tom were walking along the street, looking around. Rowling was born 31 July 1965 at Chipping Sodbury Cottage Hospital in Chipping Sodbury, Yate, Gloucesterhire England. She grew up in Tutshill, Gloucestershire, and went to school at Wyedean Comprehensive. She earned a degree in French and Classics at the University of Exeter. She worked at Amnesty International in London. The original idea for Harry Potter came to her on a train that was delayed for four hours in 1990. She moved to Portugal to teach English in 1990. She married Jorge Arantes in October 1992. They had her first child, Jessica in 1993. The marriage ended in divorce and Rowling moved to Edinburgh, Scotland. "Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone" (British version) (American version: "Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone") was first published in the United Kingdom in 1997. She married Dr. Neil Murray in 2001. She had a second child, David, in 2003, and a third, Mackenzie, in January 2005. Rowling claims inspiration from Christianity. Biochemists often draw a picture of an enzyme to use as a visual aid or map of the enzyme. This is hard to do because there may be hundreds or thousands of atoms in an enzyme. Biochemists can not draw all this detail. Instead, they use ribbon models as pictures of enzymes. Ribbon models can show the shape of an enzyme without having to draw every atom. Title: How to Act with Your Boyfriend Around His Friends Introduction: Interacting with your boyfriend's friends can sometimes be challenging, especially if you are new to his social circle or have different interests. However, building positive relationships with them is essential for maintaining harmony in your relationship. This article will provide a comprehensive guide on how to act appropriately around your boyfriend's friends while fostering healthy connections. Step 1: Understand Their Importance Explanation: Recognizing that your boyfriend values his friendships allows you to appreciate their role in his life and treat them respectfully. By doing so, you strengthen your bond with him and create an inclusive atmosphere where everyone feels valued. Key Tip: Show genuine interest in learning about his friends and understanding what they mean to him. Step 2: Be Respectful and Polite Explanation: Treating his friends kindly demonstrates maturity and emotional intelligence, which enhances your image as someone who cares about others' feelings. It also sets the stage for open communication and mutual respect between all parties involved. Key Guideline: Address his friends by their preferred names, engage in conversations, listen attentively, and respond thoughtfully. Step 3: Encourage Individual Interactions Explanation: Engaging in one-on-one conversations with his friends enables you to develop personal connections beyond group settings. These interactions promote trust and deepen your understanding of individual personalities within the group. Key Tip: Initiate side conversations during gatherings or plan activities involving just you and another friend. Step 4: Avoid Overstepping Boundaries Explanation: Being aware of personal boundaries preserves comfort levels and prevents potential conflicts. Acknowledging limits shows consideration for individual privacy and maintains a balanced dynamic among the group. Key Guideline: Refrain from discussing sensitive topics, sharing confidential information, or making assumptions without prior consent. Step 5: Support Shared Interests Explanation: Participating in shared hobbies or events creates common ground and promotes camaraderie. By showing enthusiasm for these activities, you demonstrate willingness to invest time and effort into areas important to your boyfriend and his friends. Key Tip: Attend games, concerts, or other functions together when possible, even if you don't initially share the same level of interest. Step 6: Maintain Independence Explanation: Balancing personal autonomy with coupledom ensures that both you and your boyfriend maintain separate identities outside of the relationship. This balance ultimately benefits all parties by promoting self-growth and reducing codependency. Key Guideline: Pursue individual passions, spend quality time apart, and foster independence within your own friendships. Step 7: Manage Conflicts Gracefully Explanation: Disagreements may arise due to differing opinions or misunderstandings; however, addressing issues calmly and constructively showcases emotional intelligence and problem-solving skills. Handling conflicts gracefully contributes to overall group cohesion and resilience. Key Tip: Listen actively, validate concerns, express yourself clearly, and seek compromises rather than insisting on being right. Conclusion: By following this thorough guide on acting appropriately around your boyfriend's friends, you contribute positively to his social environment and solidify your position as a supportive partner. Remember always to remain respectful, empathetic, and considerate towards others, thus ensuring harmonious interactions for years to come. [as Bot removes the lid of the container] Title: How to Get Your Sims Pregnant in The Sims 4 The Sims 4 is a life simulation game that allows players to create unique sims and control their lives. One common activity in this game is getting your sims pregnant. This can be an exciting event that leads to new challenges and opportunities. However, achieving pregnancy in The Sims 4 requires following specific steps and understanding some essential guidelines. Here's our comprehensive guide on "how to get your sims pregnant" in The Sims 4. **Table of Contents:** 1. **Creating Two Sims** 2. **Ensure Compatibility** 3. **Build Relationship** 4. **Try for Baby** 5. **Wait for Confirmation** 6. **Monitor Pregnancy Progress** 7. **Welcome the Newborn** 8. **Key Tips and Guidelines** **1. Creating Two Sims** To start with, you need two sims - one male and one female. You can either create them from scratch or choose existing ones in the gallery. When creating your sims, consider their traits, aspirations, and appearances as they will affect how they interact with each other during gameplay. **2. Ensure Compatibility** Not all sims are compatible for reproduction. For instance, same-sex couples cannot naturally conceive children without using mods or cheats. Also, teenage sims cannot get pregnant; only young adults, adults, and elders have this ability. Make sure both of your chosen sims meet these criteria before proceeding. **3. Build Relationship** Before trying for a baby, it's crucial to build a strong relationship between the two sims. They should at least be good friends or better (flirty, romantic, etc.). A high friendship score increases the chance of successful conception when attempting to 'try for baby'. To improve relationships, encourage interactions like talking, joking, giving gifts, watching TV together, having meals, and going on dates. **4. Try for Baby** Once your sims are ready, let them 'try for baby.' This action triggers a special animation indicating intimacy which may lead to pregnancy if successful. It doesn't always work on the first try, so keep trying until the 'pregnancy test' shows positive results. Remember, there's no fail state here; if unsuccessful, just keep building the relationship and trying again. **5. Wait for Confirmation** Most of the earliest works were epic poems. It is the industrial banking center of the United States. Mangrove cat snakes are one of the biggest species of cat snakes. They are snakes of tropical regions and are also known as the gold-ringed cat snake. They have distinctive small, yellow stripes on a black body and rear fangs. These snakes are considered mildly venomous and are fierce tempered. Their short rear fangs can deliver mild venom that is not dangerous to humans. Mangrove snakes are proved to be one of the hardest to capture snakes because of their slim body that are brilliantly camouflaged in their brightly sunlit, leafy habitat. Mangrove snakes have unique patterning of thin golden yellow rings on a black background. The scales around the mouth are yellow with black edges and the underbelly is blackish-blue. These snakes have a short triangular head, distinct from their neck. They are considered 'rear-fanged' snakes, a grooved tooth on either side of the rear mouth area allows these snakes to utilise their venom. These snakes very rarely leave the trees and come to the ground. They climb trees with incredible speed. On the ground they travel rapidly in a straight line but invariably seek to reach the nearest high point and quickly arrange themselves in rings. They usually curl up and spend the day motionless. These snakes will grasp branches with the lower 1/3 of their body, leaving the upper part of themselves coiled and ready to strike. These snakes are skilled swimmers. Females lay between four to 12 eggs to a clutch. It takes three to four months to hatch. Hatchlings are about 30 to 40 centimetres long. Mangrove snakes are five to six feet. They may grow to length of about eight feet. These snakes feed on small mammals, lizards, frogs, snakes and fish. Mangrove snakes are found throughout Indonesia, India, Thailand, Vietnam, Singapore, Borneo, Philippine, and Malaysia. They live in coastal mangrove swamps and humid forests, usually near water. These snakes have lifespan of over 12 years. [gasps] we're having some cookies at this tea party? The people that settled in Mesopotamia about 3500 B. C. were a short, stocky, black-haired people called the Sumerians. The area that they settled in was called Sumer. The Sumerian civilization is the earliest known civilization on Earth. For the first time, people controlled their physical environments. The umerians knew that they had to control the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. In the spring the rivers flooded, and when they receded, they left natural levees behind. The Sumerians built the levees higher and used them to keep back the floodwaters. In the summer, when the land was dry, the Sumeians poked holes in the levees. The river water ran through the holes and made irrigation channel in the soil. The Sumerians' cros were: barley, wheat, sesame, flax, fruit trees, date palms, and many different types of vegetables. An irrigation system took much planning. People had to learn to work together. In time, the Sumerians organized themselves. They set up governments to make laws. These laws were made so the Sumerian powple would know what was expected of them. They began to build cities as population grew. There was no building stone and very little lumber in the desert where the Sumeians leved. To solve this problem, Sumerians usd mud from the river mixed with crushed reeds to make bricks. They baked the bricks in the sun, and then used them to build their cties. Some great cities of Sumer wee Ur and Uruk. The Sumerians were the first to build cities in this part of the world. Each of the Sumerian city-states was made up of the city nd the farmland around it. Walls of sun-dried brick surrounded all cities. The walls had bronze gates that were opened in daylight and closed at night to keep out animals and bandits. Narrow and winding streets went from the gates to the city center. The houses of the upper class were in the center of town. The upper class was priests and merchants. Their houses wre two stories high with wooden balconis that looked out over courtyards. The courtyards provided light and air for roons. Outside walls were windowless to keep out the heat and smells of the street. Behind te upper class's houses were the hauses of the middle class. The middle class was government officials, shopkeepers, and artisans. These houses were built around open courtyards, but were only one story high. Farther out was where the lower class lived-farmers, unskilled workers, and fisherman. At the center of each Sumerian city was a temple, called a ziggurat. The word "ziggurat" meant "mountain of god" or hill of heaven. Each ziggurat was made up of a series of square levels. Each level was smaller than the one below it. Stairwaysld to the top of the colossal ziggurats, which were believed to be the home of the city's chief god. Only priests could enter this sacred area. Around the ziggurat were courts, the center of Sumerian life. Artisans worked there; children went to school there; farmers, artisans, and traders stored their goods there; and poor were fed there. The Sumerians believed that all forces of nature were alive. They viewed them a gods because they could not control them There were more than 3,000 Sumerian gods and godesses. Only priests could know the gods' will. Because of this, Sumerian priests were very powerful For example, the city's god owned all land. But the priests administered the land in the god's name Also, the priests ran schools. Schools were for the sons of the rich only. Poorer boys worked in the fields or they learned a trade. Schools were roms off the ziggurat courtyards. They were called tablet houses because they were built to teach childten how to write. The childern wrote with sharp-ended reeds on clay tablets the size of a postcard. Sumerian writing was called cuneiform. It was made up of hundreds of wedge-shaped markings. Writing in Sumerian culture developed so that people could keep track of business deals. Whenth Smrian lve i villages, the could keep track of everything easily. When they began living in cities, it became harder to keep track of everyting in their heads. To solve this problem, they developed cuneiform. When a pupil graduated from school, he became a scribe. The ziggurat, the palace, the government, or the army employed him. Although Sumerian women did not go to school, they did have many rights. They could buy and sell property, run businesses, and own and sell slaves. Although a woman handled the house's affairs when the man was away, the men were the head of the Sumerian household. He could divorce his wife by saying," You're not my wife." If he needed money, he could sell or rent his family into slavery for up to three years. The man also arranged the marraiges of his children. Children were expected to support their parents when the parents got old. They were also expected to obey older family members. Everyone was to obey the gods and priests. At first, Sumerian priests were kings of city-states. One of the most famous priest kings was Gilgamesh of Uruk. The Sumerian priest-kings received advice from a general assembly made up of free men. When war broke out, the assembly would choose one of its members to serve as leaders until the war was over, but often these leaders stayed in power even when peace had returned. By about 3000 B. C., they took their place as permanent kings. At the same time, kingship became heredity, and the world's first monarchies were established. Early forms of algebra were developed by the Babylonians and the Greek geometers such as Hero of Alexandria. However the word "algebra" is a Latin form of the Arabic word "Al-Jabr" ("casting") and comes from a mathematics book "Al-Maqala fi Hisab-al Jabr wa-al-Muqabilah", ("Essay on the Computation of Casting and Equation") written in the 9th century by a Persian mathematician, Muhammad ibn M s al-Khw rizm , who was a Muslim born in Khwarizm in Uzbekistan. He flourished under Al-Ma'moun in Baghdad, Iraq through 813-833 AD, and died around 840 AD. The book was brought into Europe and translated into Latin in the 12th century. The book was then given the name 'Algebra'. (The ending of the mathematician's name, al-Khwarizmi, was changed into a word easier to say in Latin, and became the English word "algorithm"). This article - short stories for ESL - is the second of two, giving ideas for short stories in the classroom. This kind of activity works best with pre-intermediate and intermediate level students. The first article, ESL Short Stories gives the methodology for using these stories - or one method which I've found particularly useful for giving students intensive practice in past tenses. For different ideas on using ESL Stories see here. The aim of the article: 'Short Stories for ESL' is to save you time if you need a quick story for the classroom, but are stuck on ideas. Hopefully, these stories will work as well for you as they have for me, and give your students interesting practice. For full instructions on the methodology, see the first link, above. This is a story about time management and setting priorities, which I came across on the internet. It's a nice story (and you may have heard it before, but I don't think that this matters in the context of the exercise). So here it it. A professor stood before his class. He had some items in front of him. Wordlessly, he picked up an empty jar and filled it with rocks. He asked the students if it was full. They agreed that it was. Then he poured in a container of pebbles. The students laughed. He asked the students again if it was full. They agreed that is was full. Then he picked up a container of sand, and poured it in until the jar was full. He said that the jar represents your life. The rocks are the important things: family, health, relationships etc. The pebbles are other things that matter: your job, house, car etc. The sand is everything else - the small stuff. He told the students that if you fill your jar with sand and pebbles first, there will be no room for the rocks. The message is to set your priorities; what is left is just sand and pebbles. With this story I'd write a list like this on the board: I would add any other short notes or better still, quickly drawn pictures instead, to aid both my memory, and that of the students. Then as normal we would tell the story. With an interesting or meaningful story, like this one, it's I would also discuss the meaning with the class. Another of the short stories for ESL which I found on the internet; this time on an online newspaper. This true story took place in Utah, USA. A man in Utah had recently moved house. One day he decided to explore the attic. He went up into the attic discovered $45,000 stuffed into boxes. He took the money downstairs, spread it out and counted it. At first he was tempted to keep it, but he decided that this was wrong. So he sought the children of the previous owner, who had since died, and gave them the money. He said the money wasn't ours, and that you don't often have the chance to be really honest. He told reporters that it would be a lesson to teach his children. I won't add the list of verbs, which would vary slightly from class to class anyway, but let you decide which are the important points of the story. With lower level classes I would shorten and simplify the story (and verbs used). This is a story that I came across a long time ago, and have used it with many classes. It was reported in newspapers at the time. An old woman was walking home with her shopping one day, when a young man jumped out in front of her, and tried to rob her. He grabbed hold of her bag and tried to pull it away, but she gripped his arm. He shouted for her to let go, but she held on tighter. Then she threw him to the ground. The young man screamed and let go of the bag. Then he picked himself up off the ground and ran away. She told reporters that when she had been younger she had studied judo. She said that when her husband had been alive, she had used to practice throwing him around the living room every Christmas. This is one of the stranger stories that I've come across. I found it in a book on the history of sword fighters. The story happened in 1372 in Paris, France.A French knight killed his friend after an argument. He buried his friend's body in a forest, but the dead man's dog, a very large greyhound, saw what had happened. The dog went to the house of the dead man's friend and dragged him to the forest. The dog dug up his master's body. Then, every time the dog saw the knight, he attacked the man. The dead man's friend became suspicious and he informed the king. The king decided that the knight's innocence or guilt would be decided by fighting a duel with the dog. The knight was given a lance, the dog was given an empty barrel, open at both ends, to hide in. The dog got the man by the throat, and he confessed to the murder. These are just a few examples of short stories for ESL that I've successfully used in the class. It's not difficult to find more stories to use; either personal anecdotes or stories from the news. An internet search for 'strange stories in the news,' or 'weird news,' will bring up many odd stories that can be of interest to students. Of course, eventually, it's a good idea for the students to share their own stories; which don't have to be as strange as the dog duel, but can be everyday stories in the students' lives. If you persist in using this methodology you will find that, over the months, your students' ability in telling stories will improve, they will become more fluent in using the past tense, and they will become more confident in speaking English. Today, let's learn about building a super cool gadget - a binary number incrementer! Imagine having a machine that counts up in ones and zeros like a computer does. That's what we're going to create together using simple concepts. First, let's understand binary numbers. We know how to count in our daily lives - 1, 2, 3, 4, and so on. In computers, numbers are represented differently using only two digits, 0 and 1. These are called binary numbers. So, counting in binary looks like this: 0, 1, 10, 11, 100, and so forth. You can think of each digit as a light switch - either off (0) or on (1). Now, imagine having a row of light switches, and you want to turn them all off and then flip the first switch on. To do this quickly, instead of individually flipping the switches one by one, you'd want to find a faster way. This concept applies when increasing binary numbers too! It would be nice to have a magical box where you put in a binary number, and it gives you the next number instantly. But how do we build such a box? The naive approach would be creating a custom adder for every single bit position and connecting them in series like a chain reaction. While this works fine, it gets slower when dealing with long strings of ones since the "carry" needs to travel through the entire chain before giving us the final answer. Can we improve upon this idea? Sure! Instead of adding bits sequentially, why not add multiple bits simultaneously? Here comes the exciting part-using something called a parallel prefix circuit! Think of it as several smaller circuits working together in tandem, making things run more smoothly without waiting for each other. With some clever design, these mini-circuits perform calculations at almost the same time, significantly improving speed while keeping the size reasonable. Now, doesn't that sound better than our slow chain reaction method? To wrap up, today we learned about designing a logical circuit that helps increase binary numbers more efficiently. By dividing the problem into smaller parts and letting many little circuits work together, we created a quicker solution compared to handling everything step-by-step. Next time someone mentions binary numbers or circuits, show off your newfound knowledge and leave them amazed! Title: How to Determine a Dog's Age in Human Years Introduction: Dogs age at a faster rate than humans, but determining their exact human equivalent age is not as straightforward as multiplying by seven. This method only works for the first two years of a dog's life; after that, various factors come into play such as breed size and overall health. Here's an easy-to-follow guide on how to estimate your dog's age in human years accurately. Step 1: Understand the First Two Years The common misconception of multiplying a dog's age by seven stems from the fact that during the initial developmental stages, dogs grow rapidly compared to humans. A one-year-old dog can be considered similar to a 15-year-old human because they have reached sexual maturity. By the time they turn two, they are comparable to a 24-year-old human due to further growth spurts and emotional maturing. Key Tip: Remember this rule applies mainly to medium or large breeds. Smaller breeds tend to live longer so while they may reach physical adulthood quickly, mentally and emotionally, they might still be like teenagers even when they hit two! Step 2: Consider Breed Size Post-Two Years Once your dog surpasses its second birthday, you need to take its breed size into account. Generally speaking, larger breeds age more quickly than smaller ones do. Therefore, estimating a dog's age based solely on chronological years post-two won't give you an accurate representation of their biological age. Guideline: Use these general categories for approximate equivalents: - Small breeds (under 20 lbs): Add about four years per calendar year. - Medium breeds (20-50 lbs): Add around six years per calendar year. - Large breeds (50-90 lbs): Add approximately eight years per calendar year. - Giant breeds (over 90 lbs): Add roughly ten years per calendar year. Step 3: Factor in Lifestyle & Health Conditions Just like with humans, lifestyle choices and pre-existing conditions can greatly influence a dog's lifespan. Regular exercise, balanced nutrition, routine vet checkups, and mental stimulation contribute significantly towards longevity. Conversely, obesity, lack of dental care, poor diet, stress, and chronic illnesses could potentially shorten a dog's life expectancy. Key Tips: - Keep your dog active through regular walks or playing fetch. - Feed them high-quality food suitable for their age, size, and activity level. - Schedule annual veterinary visits for preventative healthcare measures. Conclusion: While there isn't an exact science behind converting dog years to human years, using the guidelines provided here will help provide a reasonable estimation. Always remember that individual genetic makeup, environmental influences, and lifestyle habits also play crucial roles in determining a dog's 'human age.' But he can flip through the pages and stuff. When given an equation in the form y = mx + b, or slope-intercept form, we should know how to graph a line without having to compute two points on it. If we have an equation in slope-intercept form, we automatically know the slope and the y-intercept and can use that information to more quickly and efficiently graph the line. Another method of graphing a line is using a table of values or the intercepts. When you're asked to graph a line, you have three big options on how you want to approach it. The first option is to make an xy table of values, where you choose x values and you substitute them in one by one to find the corresponding y values, and then plot those points on the graph. For me that takes all day. I don't like that method. The second option you have is to use y=mx+b strategies and this is really useful if your equation is already in slope intercept form like this or if it's close to that and you just need to do a little bit of solving. This is my personal favorite method. Here's is how it goes. Your first dot goes on the y axis at the y intercept. From there you count the slope and make another point and then you just connect them. That's my favorite and that's what we're going to be practicing more in your homework these days. The third option you have is to find and connect to the x and y intercepts. The way you find the x intercept is by substituting in in y=0 and to find the y intercept you substitute in x=0. So that's a good option. Most people like this method if the equation's given to you in standard form. You always have a choice and you could always kind of barrel through any one of these methods. But one of the signs of a more advanced student is a student who knows how to choose a different method for the appropriate problem and to be able to justify why he chose that method. So that's something to keep in mind. All of these will work but for any given problem one method will be way easier than the others. That's something we're going to be practicing as you guys work through your homework problems. An' I ran the water out of it. Creating a Software Simulation Project using FlashDemo Screen Recorder ============================================================== Step 1: Download and Install FlashDemo Screen Recorder ----------------------------------------------------- To start creating your software simulation project, you need to download and install FlashDemo Screen Recorder. You can download the latest version from the official website. Once downloaded, run the installation file and follow the prompts to complete the installation process. This tool will allow you to record your screen and create a comprehensive software simulation. Key Tips and Guidelines * Make sure to choose the correct version (Windows or Mac) based on your operating system. * During installation, select the option to install additional components if necessary, such as codecs that may be required for optimal performance. Step 2: Configure Recording Settings ------------------------------------ Launch FlashDemo Screen Recorder and configure the recording settings according to your requirements. Choose the area of the screen you want to capture, set the audio source, adjust frame rate, and customize other options as needed. These configurations ensure that the recorded video meets your expectations in terms of quality and size. Key Tips and Guidelines * Select an appropriate frame rate (usually between 20-30 fps) to balance between video quality and file size. * If possible, connect a high-quality external microphone for better narration audio. Step 3: Start Recording Your Software Demonstration --------------------------------------------------- Click the 'Record' button to begin capturing your screen. Perform all the actions within the software application that you would like to include in the simulation. Speak clearly while performing these tasks so that users watching the final product understand what is happening at each stage. When finished with the demonstration, click the 'Stop' button. Key Tips and Guidelines * Practice your demonstration several times before starting the actual recording. * Consider adding text overlays during critical steps to enhance understanding. Step 4: Edit the Recorded Video ------------------------------ After recording, open the video editor provided by FlashDemo Screen Recorder. Here, you can trim unwanted parts, add transitions, insert annotations, highlight mouse clicks, and perform various editing tasks. The goal here is to refine the raw footage into a polished and engaging presentation. Key Tips and Guidelines * Use markers to quickly navigate through the timeline during editing. * Keep animations simple and subtle; they should assist learning rather than distract viewers. Step 5: Add Interactivity (Optional) ------------------------------------ FlashDemo allows you to incorporate interactivity into your simulations. For instance, you could add buttons triggering specific sections of the demo or quizzes testing user comprehension. To do this, utilize the interactive elements available in the software interface. Key Tips and Guidelines * Design interactions meaningful to the training objective. * Test each interaction thoroughly to avoid technical issues when publishing. Step 6: Publish Your Software Simulation --------------------------------------- Once satisfied with your edits, publish the project using one of the output formats supported by FlashDemo (such as HTML5, EXE, FLV). Depending on your needs, some formats might require additional plugins or players. After publication, test the output to verify everything works correctly. Key Tips and Guidelines * Preview published projects across different devices to ensure compatibility. * Save multiple copies of your project in case revisions are needed later. By following these steps meticulously, you'll have created a professional-grade software simulation project ready to engage learners! Yeah, we're going to send her to the hospital. As of 2011, schizophrenia affects around 0.3% to 0.7% of people, or 24 million people worldwide, at some point in their lives. More men are affected than women: the number of males with the disorder is 1.4 times greater than that of females. Schizophrenia usually appears earlier in men. For males the symptoms usually start from 20 to 28 years of age, and in females it is 26 to 32 years of age. Symptoms that start in childhood, middle or old age are much rarer. Despite the received wisdom that schizophrenia occurs at similar rates worldwide, its rate of likelihood varies across the world, within countries, and at the local level. The disorder causes approximately 1% of worldwide disability adjusted life years (in other words, years spent with a disability). The rate of schizophrenia varies depending on how it is defined. On this leaflet we explain the procedure for factoring quadratic expressions such as x +5 x +6. 1. Factoring quadratics You will find that you are expected to be able to factorize expressions such as x + 5 x + 6. First of all note that by removing the brackets from ( x + 2)( x + 3) we find ( x + 2)( x + 3) = x + 2 x + 3 x + 6 = x+ 5 x + 6 When we factorize x + 5 x + 6 we are looking for the answer ( x + 2)( x + 3). It is often convenient to do this by a process of educated guesswork and trial and error. Factorize x + 6 x + 5. We would like to write x + 6 x + 5 in the form First note that we can achieve the x term by placing an x in each bracket: The next place to look is the constant term in x+6 x +5, that is, 5. By removing the brackets you will see that this is calculated by multiplying the two numbers in the brackets together. We seek two numbers which multiply together to give 5. Clearly 5 and 1 have this property, although there are others. So x + 6 x +5 = ( x + 5)( x + 1) At this stage you should always remove the brackets again to The factors of x+ 6 x + 5 are ( x + 5) and ( x + 1). Factorize x - 6 x + 5. Again we try to write the expression in the form x-6 x + 5 = And again we seek two numbers which multiply to give 5. However this time 5 and 1 will not do, because using these we would obtain a middle term of +6 x as we saw in the last example. Trying 5 and 1 will do the trick. x - 6 x +5 = ( x - 5)( x - 1) You see that some thought and perhaps a little experimentation You will need even more thought and care if the coefficient of x, that is the number in front of the x, is anything other than 1. Consider the following example. Factorize 2 x + 11 x + 12. Always start by trying to obtain the correct x 2 x + 11 x +12 = Then study the constant term12. It has a number of pairs of factors, for example 3 and 4, 6 and 2 and so on. By trial and error you will find that the correct factorization is 2 x + 11 x + 12 = (2 x + 3)( x + 4) but you will only realize this by removing the brackets again. [holds out her hands for the ball] Bananas for Fluency Rationale. Students increase their oral reading fluency by participating in a readers theater and practicing it multiple times before performing it with the class. Students will also be able to recognize and read high-frequency words in the story. Students will also be able to read sight vocabulary words in the story. As the students reread their lines they will develop more fluency in their reading and be able to use their learned decoding skills to read harder words. Strips of Jack and Jill Paper with sentence I want to go out to play today Paper with word Tim on it decodable book, Jane and Babe progress charts of Monkey and tree with bananas for one-minute reads fluency checklists (1 per student) 1. We have been working hard on our reading skills. Today we are going to work on something called fluency. Fluency is being able to read fast and smooth, and with expression so that we can make the stories we read aloud more interesting. 2. The first time we read something we may not always know what the word is so we use our reading strategies to figure out the word. What is one thing that we can use? (Cover-ups) Good we can use cover-ups to figure out words we don t know. When we look at the word we cover up everything but the vowel. Then we start with the first letter of the word and read one letter at a time until the end. If we were trying to read the word Tim. First I would look at the I and realize that I says /i/. Next I would look at the first letter t that says /t/. So I would say Tttttiiiii then add the last letter m. /T/ /i/ /m/. The next time we read those sentences we will be able to figure out the words easier by using cover-ups. 3. Can anyone think of other ways to help ourselves if we get stuck on a word. (Cross-checking) Good! When we read a sentence if we read something that didnt make sense we go back and try to figure out what the word that didnt make sense would be. When we figure out what the word is we can go back and reread the sentence to make sure it all makes sense. 4. Now that we know how to figure out a word we don t know we are going to work on reading fluency. Let s look at the sentence I want to go out and play today. The first time I read this I might read it as I wwwwaaant, want to go out and pppllllaaaayyy todddaaayy. The next time I read it I might say I want to go out and play (really fast). The last time I read it I might say I want to go out and play today (with expression). The third time I read the sentence I was able to read it fast and smoothly and with expression to make it sound more interesting. 5. Now we are going to do a small readers theater in our small group of four. We are going to read Jack and Jill. (Distribute each strip of paper with one sentence on it.) First I want you to read your part to your partner three times to practice it. Your partner is going to use a checklist to see how your reading improves the more you read your part. Then your partner will read you their part 3 times. Next, you are going to take turns reading your part aloud to just me so I can make sure you are reading fluently. Lastly, we will read the whole story together as a readers theater. 6. After we have done readers theater students will return to their seats and read silently a book of their choice. One at a time I will call them up to do one minute reads of Jane and Babe. They will read the book 3 times. I will show them their progress by moving a monkey up a tree to get the bananas. If they reach the goal I have set the monkey will get the bananas. This will help me assess how much they improve after each reading. Jack and Jill Text: Reader 1: Jack and Jill went up the hill, Reader 2: To fetch a pail of water, Reader 1: Jack fell down and broke his crown Reader 2: And Jill came tumbling after. Readers LongVowels: Jane and Babe. (1990). Readers Theatre K-3. Cholesterol does many important jobs in a cell. Cholesterols main function is as a structural component of cell membranes. It is also the starting material for bile acids that are made by the liver and used to digest fats, and for steroid hormones. However, it is best known for something bad that it does. High levels of cholesterol can lead to atherosclerosis. This is an inflammatory disease of artery walls in which white blood cells invade the vessel wall and become engorged with cholesterol and other lipids. PLANTS AND SUNLIGHT Biological Science - Plant Needs - Sunlight The Student will be able to prove that a plant needs light in order for it to develop correctly. seeds (any alternative seed will do), potting soil, flower pots of plastic containers, box, aluminum foil, houseplant, two plants the same size BACKGROUND INFORMATION: Plants need food and can make their own food. But they need things from which to make their food. Green plants need carbon dioxide from the air, water, and minerals from the soil, and energy from the sun. Only in light can a green plant make food. The process of food making is called photosynthesis. In photosynthesis, the carbon dioxide and water are changed to carbohydrates and oxygen. Food can only be made in the presence of chlorophyll. Chlorophyll is the substance responsible for a plant's green color. When a green plant is deprived of sunlight, it soon loses its chlorophyll. It cannot make food, so it dies. the sunflower seeds overnight. Then plant three sunflower seeds in each pot and put them near the window. Water the pots well. Now cover one pot with the cardboard the first true leaves appear on the plants that are uncovered, remove the box from the other pot. with the students what happened to the plants. Why did the sunflower seed under the box not grow? Do plants need light to grow? Do they need light to remain healthy? - When a plant gets no sunlight, it cannot make food. Without food the plant will die. Use two plants of the same size. Cover one with a box or place it in a closet to block the sunlight. The plant that did not receive sunlight will lose some of is green color. It will not look as healthy as the other plant. Make sure it now receives sun and watch for it to return to health. - Put a potato in a dark, warm spot for several weeks. What happens to the potato? If left in the dark for several months, will the potato die? Try it. three of four seeds in the bottom of a shallow bowl and place a wet sponge over them, Keep them damp. When the seeds sprout, watch to see what happens to them. Turn the dish. Do the seedlings turn toward the light? - Put a rock or a board on a patch of grass, leave it for two weeks. Take the rock off and observe. What has happened to the grass? Is it a different color from the surrounding grass? Has the grass died? Now leave he rock or board off the grass. Does the green color return to the grass? a plant log about one of the experiments. the students make up an activity of their own with plants and sunshine. Have the students keep their plant long using a word processing sheet or have them create a chart in excel to fill in daily.
