[ROM looked for the ball behind him] [continue to look out the window into the backyard] Title: How to Enable Dark Mode on Groove Music Introduction: Dark mode is a display setting that uses darker colors for the user interface, which can help reduce eye strain in low-light conditions. This tutorial will guide you through enabling dark mode on Microsoft's Groove Music app on Windows 10. By following these steps, you can enjoy your music while giving your eyes a break from bright screens. Step 1: Open Settings App Click on the Start button (Windows logo) located at the bottom left corner of your screen or press the Windows key on your keyboard. Once the Start Menu appears, click on the gear icon labeled 'Settings'. If you cannot find the gear icon directly, type 'settings' into the search bar and select the corresponding result. Key tip: The Settings app allows users to customize various aspects of their system according to personal preferences. It gathers all configuration options previously scattered across different Control Panel sections. Step 2: Navigate to Personalization Section From the main Settings window, look for 'Personalization' among the listed categories and click it. This action opens another page dedicated to modifying visual elements such as background, lock screen, colors, etc. Guideline: Changing color schemes involves multiple factors; thus, they are grouped under one category within settings. Personalizing your device enhances usability since it adapts to individual needs and tastes. Step 3: Access Colors Option On the left side panel of the Personalization section, scroll down until you see 'Colors', then click it. Here, you can manage accent colors, default apps mode, and other related features. Step 4: Choose Default App Mode Under the 'Choose your color' heading, locate the dropdown menu titled 'Default app mode'. Clicking this option reveals two choices - Light and Dark. Select 'Dark'. Upon selection, the entire window switches to a dark theme instantly. Key Tip: Immediately after changing the default app mode, some applications might still retain their previous light themes due to caching. Restarting affected programs should resolve this issue. Step 5: Verify Groove Music Application Theme Launch the Groove Music application either via Cortana/Search function or by browsing through installed programs in the Start Menu. Observe whether the new dark theme has been applied correctly throughout the application. Common Issue & Solution: Some users may notice that despite selecting the dark mode, certain areas within the Groove Music app remain untouched by the change. To fix this inconsistency, try restarting the Groove Music app entirely. If the problem persists, consider logging out and signing back into your account. Conclusion: With just a few clicks, you have successfully enabled dark mode on the Groove Music app. Enjoy listening to your favorite tunes without worrying about harsh lighting affecting your comfort. Remember, adjusting your device's appearance based on environmental conditions promotes healthier digital habits. Well you take sixty-eight and divide it into fifty and eighteen Ross. How to Draw Straight Lines on PowerPoint 2007 Slides PowerPoint provides a Line button so you can draw straight lines on your slides. With your PowerPoint slide open, choose a location for your line, then follow these steps: Click the Line button in the Shapes group on the Insert tab. The button's icon looks just like you'd think it would - a straight line. Point the cursor to where you want the line to start. This is the beginning point for your line. Click and drag the cursor to where you want the line to end. This is the end point for your line. Release the mouse button when you reach your destination. After you've drawn the shape, the Ribbon displays the Drawing Tools tab. After you've drawn a line, you can adjust it by clicking it and then dragging the handles that appear on each end of the line. Remember that you can force a line to be perfectly horizontal or vertical by holding down the Shift key while you draw. If you hold the Shift key and drag diagonally while you draw the line, the line will be constrained to perfect 45-degree angles. You know what the square and the triangle... Title: How to Help Non-Members in Animal Jam Animal Jam is an online playground where players can adopt pets, design dens, play games, interact with friends, and learn about animals and their habitats. While some features are exclusive to members only, there are still many ways non-members can enjoy this virtual world. As a member or even as another non-member, you can help newcomers have a great experience in Animal Jama by following these steps. ### Step 1: Be Welcoming When you come across a non-member in Animal Jam, greet them warmly! A simple 'Hi' or 'Hello' goes a long way, especially if they seem lost or unsure of what to do next. This friendly gesture establishes a positive atmosphere and encourages communication between players. By initiating contact, you also create opportunities for forming friendships that transcend membership statuses. Key Tip: Use appropriate language at all times; avoid using slang or abbreviated words unfamiliar to younger audiences. Guideline: Always be respectful when addressing others, regardless of age or background. ### Step 2: Offer Assistance If someone appears confused or hesitant while navigating through Animal Jam, ask if they need any assistance. They might require guidance on finding certain areas within the game, understanding its mechanics, or learning more about specific aspects of the platform. You may provide helpful information such as directing them to popular locations like Coral Canyons, Kimbara Outback, or Appondale. Key Tip: Familiarize yourself with various parts of Animal Jam so you can offer accurate directions and advice. Guideline: Never force your help upon anyone; ensure they feel comfortable accepting your assistance before proceeding. ### Step 3: Share Knowledge Non-members have limited access to several features compared to paying subscribers. However, sharing knowledge about free content and activities available to everyone can significantly enhance their gaming experience. For example, inform them about daily spins, mini-games, den decoration contests, and other social events open to both members and non-members alike. Key Tip: Keep up-to-date with updates and changes made to Animal Jam to maintain current knowledge. Guideline: Avoid discussing paid content around non-members; instead, focus on promoting accessible experiences. ### Step 4: Encourage Participation Participate in group activities alongside non-members to encourage engagement and inclusivity. Examples include joining multiplayer games, attending parties hosted by fellow jammers, or collaboratively completing quests found throughout the map. These shared experiences foster camaraderie among participants and make for enjoyable memories. Key Tip: Choose activities suitable for all ages and preferences to cater to a diverse audience. Guideline: Refrain from dominating conversations or taking control during group sessions; allow equal opportunity for every participant to contribute ideas and opinions. ### Step 5: Report Misconduct Should you encounter instances of bullying, harassment, or rule violations towards non-members (or any player), report the offending party immediately. Utilizing the reporting feature ensures a safer environment for everyone involved and promotes ethical behavior within the community. Key Tip: Gather evidence (e.g., screenshots) prior to submitting reports for faster resolution. Guideline: Remember to remain impartial during disputes; do not take sides without proper investigation. By consistently applying these steps, you will contribute positively to the Animal Jam community, ensuring a welcoming and supportive space for non-members to explore and enjoy their time spent in this vibrant virtual universe. Title: How to Express Your Love Over the Phone Introduction: Expressing love over the phone can be challenging since you cannot rely on physical touch or facial expressions. However, with careful planning and emotional expression, you can make your loved one feel cherished and valued. This wiki-style guide will help you convey your feelings effectively and create memorable moments despite the distance. Step 1: Find a Quiet and Comfortable Space Find a quiet space where you can talk without interruptions or distractions. A comfortable environment allows both individuals to focus on their emotions and engage fully in conversation. By eliminating background noise, your partner can hear you clearly and absorb every word you say. Key Tip: Use headphones if necessary to ensure better audio quality and privacy during intimate conversations. Step 2: Schedule a Call in Advance Schedule a specific time for your call so that both parties are prepared emotionally and mentally. Giving advance notice also ensures that neither party is preoccupied with other tasks, leading to more meaningful discussions. Additionally, scheduled calls provide structure and routine, which further strengthens relationships. Guideline: Be considerate of different time zones when scheduling international calls. Step 3: Start with Small Talk Begin by discussing light topics such as daily routines or recent events before delving into deeper subjects like expressing love. Easing into serious talks creates a relaxed atmosphere and prevents unnecessary pressure. Moreover, small talk establishes common ground and builds anticipation towards heartfelt confessions. Key Tip: Avoid controversial or sensitive issues during initial stages of the conversation to maintain positivity and harmony. Step 4: Speak from the Heart When sharing your affection, speak honestly and openly about why you appreciate them. Describe situations where they made you happy, supported you, or simply brightened up your day. Authentic stories demonstrate genuine emotion and foster deep connections between partners. Guideline: Focus on the person's qualities rather than material possessions or superficial traits while expressing admiration. Step 5: Listen Actively Active listening involves paying full attention to what your partner says, asking relevant questions, and providing thoughtful responses. Show empathy by acknowledging their thoughts and feeling appreciated. Active listening strengthens bonds and demonstrates respect and understanding. Key Tip: Paraphrase or summarize your partner's statements occasionally to confirm comprehension and encourage open communication. Step 6: Use Appropriate Language Choose words carefully when communicating your affections. Opt for positive language and avoid negative phrases that may unintentionally hurt your partner's feelings. Using endearing terms and romantic phrases appropriately conveys sincerity and passion. Guideline: Always ask for permission before using pet names or personal nicknames, especially if you recently started dating. Step 7: Plan Virtual Activities Together Plan shared activities such as watching movies simultaneously, playing online games together, or cooking dinner at the same time. These experiences bring couples closer even when miles apart. Furthermore, engaging in joint endeavors fosters teamwork and solidifies partnerships. Key Tip: Choose activities that align with both partners' interests and preferences to maximize enjoyment and engagement. Step 8: End with Affirmation Conclude your conversation by reiterating your love and appreciation. Reassuring your significant other of your commitment provides comfort and security. Lastly, schedule another call or discuss plans for future interactions to keep the relationship thriving. Guideline: Consistency is vital; regular communication reinforces love and trust within relationships. Then mash the button then mash the handle. [Maisie plays with puppet. Here's an educational piece related to the snippet above that could be appropriate for grade-school students: --- Have you ever played with patio blocks before? They're fun little hexagonal shapes that can fit together perfectly, like puzzle pieces. Imagine using these blocks to create a border around a beautiful garden. The more blocks you add, the bigger the garden gets! But have you ever wondered how to calculate the exact size of the garden, based on the number of blocks used? Well, let's explore this idea together. First, let's think about the shape of each individual block. It's a regular hexagon, which means all six sides are the same length. Since we want to know the area of the garden, let's figure out the area of one single block first. To do this, imagine dividing the hexagon into smaller triangles. Each triangle will share one side with another triangle, forming a kind of star pattern inside the hexagon. How many triangles will you see in total? Count them - there should be six triangles arranged in a circle. Now, since all those triangles are identical (because our hexagon is regular), let's focus on just one of them. What's its base length? Yes, exactly! One side of the original hexagon. And what's its height? You got it again; it's also one side of the hexagon, but remember, we need to consider the slant height, not the horizontal or vertical distance. Once you find the base and height of the triangle, you can easily calculate its area, which happens to be half the product of its base times its height. But wait, don't forget that we have six such triangles covering our entire hexagon without any gaps! So, simply multiply the area of one triangle by six, and voila - now you know the area of a single patio block. Next up, let's think about lining up multiple blocks along the edges of the garden. As shown in the picture, connecting n blocks per side forms a neat pattern, leaving no spaces between them. Now comes the exciting part - calculating the total area covered by all those connected blocks. Here's where things get interesting. Instead of adding up the areas of each block individually (which would take forever), let's use some clever math tricks. By carefully analyzing the way the blocks connect, you may notice something special happening at the corners - a repeating sequence emerges! This makes calculating the total area much easier than expected. Finally, after doing some arithmetic magic, you'll end up finding the area of the garden enclosed by the path, expressed as m*( 3/2) square units. Mmm doesn't that sound cool? Just remember, m represents a positive integer value determined by the specific layout of your blocks. And guess what? With practice, you can even learn to compute larger gardens' sizes, like one having 202 blocks along its edge! Wouldn't that make for a massive garden? So go ahead, grab some friends, collect some cardboard boxes, scissors, markers, and start creating your own imaginary gardens using similar techniques! Explore different patterns, count vertices and edges, and most importantly, enjoy the process of learning mathematics through playful activities. Happy gardening! I don't know which one she prefers. Thermal energy is the type of energy that a thing has because of its temperature. In thermodynamics, thermal energy is the internal energy present in a system in a state of thermodynamic equilibrium because of its temperature. That is, heat is defined as a spontaneous "flow of energy" (energy in transit) from one object to another, caused by a difference in temperature between two objects; therefore, objects do not possess heat. Music continues from neighboring apartment.] Hello young learners! Today, we're going to talk about changing coordinates using something called the "identity function." You may already know what a coordinate is from learning about graphs on the Cartesian plane. A point's coordinates tell us where it is located on the grid. But what happens when we need to switch between different sets of coordinates? That's where our friend, the identity function, comes in handy! Let's imagine two special sets of coordinates, which we'll call ${\beta^{'}}$ and ${\beta}$. In this example, let's say: * Set ${\beta^{'}}$ has coordinates at points $(0,10)$ and $(5,0)$. * Set ${\beta}$ has coordinates at points $(-1,3)$ and $(2,-1)$. Now, suppose we want to convert a point's ${\beta^{'}}$ coordinates into its corresponding ${\beta}$ coordinates. To do so, we'll create something called a "change of coordinate matrix," denoted by ${[I]_{{\beta}^{'}}^{\beta}}$. This magical matrix will allow us to transform one set of coordinates into another easily. To build this matrix, follow these steps: 1. Write down the identity function (a fancy name for a rule that leaves things unchanged) with respect to the ${\beta^{'}}$ coordinates. The identity function tells us that $(0,10)$ becomes $(0,10)$, and $(5,0)$ remains $(5,0)$. 2. Now, express each resulting vector in terms of the ${\beta}$ coordinates. We do this just like converting coordinates back and forth between standard form and polar form-by solving systems of equations or plugging values into formulas. * For $(0,10)$, we find that it corresponds to the first vector in ${\beta}$, i.e., $(-1,3)$, multiplied by the scalar value $30$. So, we write it as $30(-1,3)$. * Similarly, for $(5,0)$, it matches up with the second vector in ${\beta}$, i.e., $(2,-1)$, scaled by $-5$. Thus, it can be represented as $-5(2,-1)$. 3. Arrange these new expressions as column vectors in a matrix. Remember, matrices are just arrays of numbers arranged in rows and columns. Our matrix looks like: {{30,\; -5\choose-10,\;10}} And there you have it! With this matrix, you can now change coordinates from ${\beta^{'}}$ to ${\beta}$ effortlessly. Just remember to multiply your original ${\beta^{'}}$ coordinate vector by this matrix to obtain its equivalent representation in ${\beta}$ coordinates. Have fun exploring coordinate changes! In "The Canon of Medicine", Avicenna (Ibn Sina) said that treatment should involve cutting out all diseased tissue. This included the use of amputation (removing a part of the body completely) or removing veins that ran in the direction of the tumor. Septal defects are problems with the structure of the heart. They are present at birth. Septal defects are located on the inside of the heart. They are on a wall that separates the chambers of the heart. There are two upper chambers of the heart called atrium. Two lower chambers of the heart are called ventricles. In a healthy heart, the blood flows from the body to the right atrium. The blood then goes into the right ventricle. The ventricle pumps this blood to the lungs to pick up fresh oxygen. The blood then returns to the left side of the heart. It enters the left atrium first, then down to the left ventricle. The ventricle pushes the blood out to the rest of the body. Septal defects allow the blood to move between the left and right chambers. The blood most often moves from the left side of the heart into the right side. This means that blood that has just returned from the lungs may end up being sent right back to the lungs. As a result, both the heart and lungs have to work harder than they need to. There are three main types of septal defects: - Atrial septal defect (ASD)-a hole in the wall between the two upper chambers (atrium) of the heart - Ventricular septal defect (VSD)-a hole in the wall between the two lower chambers (ventricles) of the heart - Atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD)-a combination of ASD, VSD, and problems with opening between chambers (called valves) The stress of pushing extra blood to the lungs may lead to heart failure. The following information applies to all three of these defects except where noted. In most cases the cause is not known. However, in a small number of cases, a defect may be caused by: - Family history of congenital heart defects - Exposure to a viral infection, drugs, or alcohol during pregnancy For most, there are no known factors that increase your baby's chance of septal defects. Some medications during pregnancy have been linked to an increased risk of septal defects. These medicines include valproic acid for seizure disorder . Many people with ASD or VSD do not have symptoms. Large defects and AVSD may cause: - Shortness of breath - Getting tired easily - Poor growth - High blood pressure in the lungs and possibly damage to the blood vessels in the lungs (in VSD and AVSD) An ASD may be found during a regular exam. The doctor may hear a heart murmur . This is a sound caused by an abnormal blood flow through a valve in the heart. In this case it is the extra large amount of blood moving through the right side of the heart. If your child's doctor suspects a heart defect, an echocardiogram may be done. An echocardiogram uses sound waves to create images of the heart. It can be used to show the size and shape of the hole in the heart. Other tests may include: Treatment may depend on the type and size of defect. There may be some treatment steps for any complications. Treatment options may include: ASD treatment options include: - About 40% of all ASDs will close on their own during the first year of life. This is more likely to occur with small defects. - An ASD that still exists at age 2 is unlikely to ever close on its own. If it is not closed in childhood, it may cause problems in adulthood. - Surgery may be recommended in children with ASD's past age 2 years. - Some ASDs can be closed without surgery. A device is placed in the hole with cardiac catheterization . This is a process that send the device to the heart through a large blood vessel. VSD treatment options include: - Many VSDs will close on their own during the first year of life. This is more likely to occur with small defects. - Small VSDs that do not close rarely cause problems. - Medium and large VSDs may cause problems. They may need supportive treatment in the first few months of life. - Surgery may be needed in children with defects that cause symptoms or do not close after 1 year. AVSD treatment options include: - Most infants with AVSD will have symptoms. Therefore they will need treatment. - Medication may be needed. They can help the heart beat strongly, keep the heart rate regular, or decrease the amount of fluid in the blood flow. - Physical activity may need to be limited. - Surgery to close the defect. - congestive heart failure-infants with signs of congestive heart failure may need to take medicine. The type of surgery used most often for septal defects is open-heart surgery. The hole is closed with stitches or a patch. In AVSD, the abnormal valves are also repaired. Living With Septal Defects Certain septal defects may increase the risk of infections in the heart. You may need to take antibiotics before certain medical and dental procedures to decrease the risk of this infection. Check with your doctor to see if you need to do this. If you do need to take antibiotics, ask your doctor to explain when they may be needed. There are no general guidelines for preventing septal defects. One type of AVSD is strongly associated with Down syndrome . Programs have been developed to help some parents reduce their risk of having a baby with this condition. Certain maternal health conditions or medications may affect the baby's health. Talk to your doctor before considering pregnancy. - Reviewer: Michael Woods - Review Date: 09/2012 - - Update Date: 00/92/2012 - On Monday, he ate through one apple, but he was still hungry! [moves yellow wand over the coins on the table; they don't stick] The intercept of a line is a Point in a graph which intersects either x axis or y axis. It can not cut both axes at a time. Now we will see how to find the line intercept with the help of formula: If we have a point on the line and the Slope of the line then the formula for finding the Intercept of a line is: b = r - ms, where 'm' represents the Slope of the line. And 'r' and's' are the coordinates of any point on the line. A number which measure the steepness of a line is known as Slope of a line. Generally slope of a line is denoted by m. It changes the value of y for small change in the 'x' along the line. Now we will see the formula for finding the slope of the line; Slope = m = Py - Qy , Px - Qx Where 'x' is the coordinate of point 'P' and 'y' is the coordinates of point 'Q'. Now we will see how to find the intercept of a line. Suppose we have the value of 'x' and 'y' coordinates are 50 and 35 and slope of line is 2.5 then we can find the intercept of a line. We know that the formula for finding the intercept of a line: b = r - ms; Here the value of 'r' is 35 and the value of 's' is 50; When we put the value of 'r' and 's' in the given formula we get intercept of a line. b = r - ms; b = 35 - 2.5 * 50; b = 35 - 125; On further solving we get: b = -90; So the intercept of a line is -90. The shortest distance measured between two points is known as straight line. If a line contains any curve then line is not straight. In mathematical Geometry a Straight Line is obtained by joining the two points. Social democracy is a kind of capitalism that tries to mix parts of socialism with capitalism. It is not a form of socialism, but shares some ideas with it. In this system, despite there still being private property, the government takes money from the rich and gives it to the poor to reduce inequality, usually in the form of social programs. While the intentions of social democracy and socialism can be similar or shared, social democracy keeps the capitalist system intact, and slightly reforms it. Socialism would mean completely getting rid of the capitalist system. Social democracy is often confused with democratic socialism due to the similar names and having the same short term goals. The biggest difference is social democrats want to stop reforming capitalism when they think their reforms are good enough, but democratic socialists will not stop until capitalism is gone. Some examples of social democracies are the Scandinavian countries. Orlando has a humid subtropical climate ("Cfa" in the Koeppen climate classification). Summers are hot and wet; winters are warm with moderate rainfall. It's [metal pieces] charged to the metal. Say I mean I like the garlic toast, huh? What do you want to write in the letter? Mean, Median, Mode, and Range The mean of a group of numbers is the average of the numbers. eg., 3, 5, 6, 8, 13. Add all the numbers together (3+5+6+8+13=35) then divide the total (35) by the number of numbers added together (5), so 35 5 = 7. The mean is 7. The median of a group of numbers is the number that is exactly in the middle when the numbers are arranged numerically. eg., 3, 5, 6, 8, 12. The physical middle number is 6. (If the set contains an even number of numbers, the median is the still the middle, eg., 6 & 7. The median is 6.5) The mode of a group of numbers is the number that appears most often. eg., 3, 5, 5, 6, 8, 12. The number that is the most common is 5. The mode is 5. (If there are several numbers that are tied for being the mode, then they are all the mode.) The range of a group of numbers is the difference between the biggest number and the smallest number. eg., 3, 5, 6, 8, 12. The range is 12 - 3 = 9. See our Teaching Resources for Mean, Median, Mode, and Range teaching and practice worksheets. Return to our Math Skills Index for more math information and ideas. Title: How to Draw Jar Jar Binks from Star Wars Introduction: Jar Jar Binks is a beloved character from the Star Wars franchise, known for his unique appearance and mannerisms. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing Jar Jar Binks, providing detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. By following this tutorial, you'll be able to create an accurate representation of the Gungan outcast. Step 1: Outline the Head Shape Begin by sketching an oval shape for the head. Make sure the top portion is slightly wider than the bottom to represent Jar Jar's elongated skull. Keep your lines light as they are only serving as guides that can be erased later. Step 2: Define Facial Features Divide the oval horizontally into three equal parts using faint lines. These divisions will help place eyes, nose, and mouth accurately. Then draw two large almond-shaped eyes just above the middle line. Add small circles inside these shapes for pupils. Step 3: Add Eyebrows and Nose Just above each eye, add curved eyebrows with pointed ends facing upwards. For the nose, draw a wide 'U' shape below the eyes but above the lower horizontal line. It should extend past the vertical centerline. Step 4: Create the Mouth Underneath the lower horizontal line, draw a broad half-circle representing the upper lip. Below it, sketch another smaller curve for the lower lip. Remember, keep all lines loose at this stage. Step 5: Form the Oval Ears On either side of the head, draw two overlapping teardrop shapes touching the head outline near the eyebrows. The outer edge of each ear should have a flat surface connecting to the head while the inner part has a rounded end close to the cheek area. Step 6: Sketch Body Structure Erase unnecessary guidelines leaving behind clean facial features. Now we move onto the body. Underneath the chin, draw a short rectangular box - this forms the base of the neck. From there, extend two angled lines downward forming the torso. Note that the left line angles more outward creating a sloping shoulder effect. Step 7: Detail Clothing Jar Jar typically wears simple robes. To depict this, draw two parallel wavy lines starting from under the chest running down to the knees. Above these lines, create a folded flap hanging over one shoulder. Step 8: Illustrate Legs & Feet From the bottom of the robe, draw two bent legs ending in long narrow feet similar to those of a bird or frog. Be mindful of perspective; if viewed from the front, both feet should point towards us. Step 9: Finalize Details Go back to refine any areas needing improvement. Pay attention to proportions, ensuring everything aligns correctly. Once satisfied, darken necessary lines giving them definition and solidifying form. Don't forget to include gills on either side of the neck! Conclusion: Congratulations! You've successfully drawn Jar Jar Binks. With patience and practice, anyone can learn to draw their favorite characters. As always, remember to take breaks during long drawing sessions to avoid fatigue and maintain accuracy throughout the entire piece. Happy drawing! You want me to bang with you? Things to try at home while the school is closed if you are in KS1 Physical Development Opportunities Key Stage 1 Have a go at some of the activities below and see how well you can do!!! Remember, it's all about setting yourself a personal challenge and enjoying what you do. 1. See how long you can skip without stopping. - Set yourself a target eg. 10 skips, 20 skips, 30 or more. - How long can you skip for, if you can get someone to time you? 2. Practise throwing and catching a ball, on your own or with a friend. - See if you can do it with one hand. - See how many times you can do it without dropping it. - How high can you throw it and catch it. - Can you clap when the ball is in the air? How many times? 3. Practise some of the things you have been doing in gymnastics. Remember to always be in control and be safe. 4. Practise your swimming strokes. - Set yourself a distance challenge. - Ask a friend to time you over a distance of your choice. 5. If you have an opportunity practice bouncing or kicking a ball with a friend. 6. Ride your bike, scooter, roller blades or have a bounce on your trampoline. Stay fit, stay healthy and have FUN! ------------ no comments ----------- And haven't we seen Davey and Goliath? Title: How to Change the Color on a Sylvania Bluetooth Speaker Color Changing Lamp (SP629) Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the color on your Sylvania Bluetooth Speaker with a built-in color changing lamp model number SP629. The speaker features a multi-color LED lamp that can be customized to suit your mood or decor. By following these steps, you'll learn how to navigate the intuitive control panel and make adjustments to the lamp's colors effortlessly. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Familiarize yourself with the control panel. Understanding the layout and function of each button is essential for successful operation. 2. Make sure your speaker is powered on before attempting any changes to the color settings. 3. Remember that some buttons may serve multiple functions depending on how long they are pressed. Be mindful of this as you progress through the steps. 4. This tutorial assumes that your device is functioning properly and does not require troubleshooting. If you encounter issues unrelated to color selection, consult the user manual or contact Sylvania support for assistance. Step 1: Power On Your Device To begin, ensure that your Sylvania Bluetooth Speaker is turned on. Press the power button located at the top of the unit until the display illuminates. A solid white light indicates that the device is ready for pairing via Bluetooth, while a pulsating blue light means the device is already connected to another source. Step 2: Access Color Mode Locate the 'Mode' button on the control panel, which typically has an icon resembling overlapping squares or rectangles. Press the mode button briefly once; doing so should cycle the lamp through various preset modes such as fade, strobe, flash, smooth, and static. Release the button when you reach your preferred mode. Step 3: Select Static Color Mode If you wish to choose a single color rather than using one of the presets mentioned above, press and hold down the 'Mode' button until the lamp switches to static color mode. This change might take several seconds. Once reached, proceed to Step 4. Step 4: Choose Your Desired Color Now that you're in static color mode, pressing the 'Volume +' button will cycle through different available colors. Each time you press the button, the next color in sequence will appear. Continue pressing until you find the hue that suits your needs. Keep in mind that certain shades like pastels may require more precise navigation within the spectrum. Step 5: Adjust Brightness Level (Optional) Should you desire further customization, you can also modify the brightness level of the chosen color. While still in static color mode, press and release the 'Play/Pause' button. You'll notice the intensity of the light decreasing gradually. Repeat this process to increase brightness again if needed. Step 6: Save Settings & Exit Once satisfied with your new color setting, simply wait approximately 10 seconds without making additional adjustments. After this period, the device will automatically save your preferences and exit color editing mode. At this point, feel free to resume playback of music or other audio content. Conclusion: Changing the color on your Sylvania Bluetooth Speaker Color Changing Lamp (SP629) offers an easy way to personalize your listening experience and create a unique ambiance. With patience and practice, mastering these controls will become second nature, enabling you to switch between different lighting effects quickly and seamlessly. Inequalities are very similar, with only a few differences: - It's not a line of solutions as in a linear equation; it is a solid or dashed boundary line that shows on which side all the solutions are. - Shade above or below the boundary line, showing on which side all the solutions are. - Change the direction of the inequality (>, <) if you divide by a negative number. Check your work by using (0,0) as a test point. This will help you know if your answer is correct, and if you forgot to change the direction of the inequality. Title: How to Delete a Discord Channel on an Android Device Discord is a popular communication platform that allows users to create servers for various communities, clubs, or groups. Each server can have multiple channels, where users can text, voice chat, share files, and more. As a server owner or administrator, you may need to delete a channel if it's no longer needed. This tutorial will guide you through deleting a Discord channel using your Android device with clear explanations, helpful tips, and best practices. **Time required:** 2-3 minutes **Tools needed:** A Discord account, access to a server as an admin or owner, and an Android device with the latest version of the Discord app installed. 1. **Open the Discord App**: First, locate the Discord app icon (a blue game controller) on your Android device and tap on it to open the application. If you haven't signed in yet, enter your credentials and log into your account. *Tip*: Make sure you are connected to the internet via WiFi or mobile data since Discord requires connectivity to function properly. 2. **Navigate to Your Server**: After opening Discord, you should see a list of all the servers you belong to. Scroll down until you find the one containing the channel you want to delete, then tap on its name to enter the server. *Guideline*: Remember only admins and owners can manage and edit channels within their respective servers. Check whether you possess sufficient permissions before attempting to delete any channels. 3. **Access the Channel List**: Once inside the server, look at the top right corner of your screen for three horizontal lines ("hamburger menu"). Tap this button to reveal the sidebar. Here, you will see different categories like Text Channels, Voice Channels, Categories, etc., depending upon your server settings. 4. **Select the Channel to Be Deleted**: Now, scroll down the list of channels under the appropriate category till you reach the target channel. Long press the channel name (press and hold) to bring up contextual options related to the selected channel. 5. **Choose 'Delete Channel' Option**: Among the displayed choices, select the 'i' (info) icon located near the bottom left corner of the popup window. On the next page, you will observe several details about the channel; swipe upwards to view more info. At the end of the list, click 'Delete Channel.' Confirm by tapping 'OK' when prompted. *Key Tip*: Double-check the spelling of the channel name while confirming the deletion process because once deleted, there is NO UNDO option available! 6. **Verify the Removal**: To ensure the successful removal of the channel, go back to the main server screen. The deleted channel should now be missing from the list under its corresponding category. *Congratulations!* You have successfully removed an unwanted Discord channel on your Android device. Keep in mind that proper management and organization of channels contribute significantly towards maintaining healthy community engagement. Always consider reorganizing or archiving channels instead of outright deletion whenever possible. [Nina takes out a toy and starts to throw it at Linda] Title: Explain English Idioms to People Who Don't Speak English Idioms are phrases or expressions that have a figurative meaning different from the literal meaning of their words. They can be challenging for non-native speakers to understand because they don't always make sense when translated directly into other languages. Here is a step-by-step guide on how to explain English idioms to people who don't speak English. ### Step 1: Define an Idiom Before explaining any idiom, first define what an idiom is. Make sure the learner understands that these are fixed phrases whose meanings cannot be deduced by adding up the individual meanings of each word. Key Tip: Use simple language while defining idioms so that learners grasp the concept easily. ### Step 2: Provide Context Context plays a crucial role in understanding idioms. Whenever possible, provide examples using situations or stories that will help illustrate the context in which the idiom might be used. This makes it easier for listeners to relate and remember the idiom. Guideline: The more relatable and familiar the context, the better the chance of comprehension. ### Step 3: Break Down the Meaning After setting the stage with context, break down the actual meaning of the idiom. Explain how the phrase's implied meaning differs from its literal interpretation. Example: If you're explaining the idiom "break a leg," you could say, "It doesn't really mean someone should physically harm their legs. Instead, it's wishing good luck before a performance." ### Step 4: Give Real Life Examples Using real-life scenarios, demonstrate how and when this idiom would typically be said. This reinforces the learning process and shows the relevance of knowing such phrases. Real Life Example: You might tell them, "If your friend was about to give a big presentation at work, you could say 'Break a leg!' instead of saying 'Good luck,' which is more commonly used in everyday conversations." ### Step 5: Practice Using the Idiom Have the learner practice using the new idiom in sentences. Encourage creativity but also ensure they stick closely to the defined usage. Practice Sentences: Ask them to create sentences like, "I need to _______ (hit the books) tonight if I want to pass my exam tomorrow." Or "Don't worry, I already __________ (crossed that bridge) yesterday." ### Step 6: Reiterate and Review Regularly Like vocabulary building, regular review is essential for mastery. Revisit previously learned idioms regularly to reinforce understanding and promote recall. Review Tips: Create flashcards, quizzes, or conversation prompts around these idioms to facilitate review. Remember, patience is vital when teaching idioms since they represent one of the subtler aspects of language acquisition. It takes time for non-native speakers to become comfortable using them correctly. Is this where we are going to sleep? At first, for younger children, you can count the number of each denomination you have on hand. And, as you move up the currency you can show how each is related to the others. Another important concept to teach here grouping similar coins and bills. One way to reinforce this grouping, is to count more than $2 worth of pennies, nickels and/or dimes. Let your child count until half of the coins have been counted, then distract him/her to read a few pages of his/her favorite book, better if it relates to counting. After that task has been completed, return your child to continue counting the coins. He/she will probably start over, and here is where you step in to show your child how to group the coins in separate piles; doing so makes it easy to return to the task and later to actually find the value of all So, a penny represents 1, a nickel represents 5 (pennies) a dime represents 10 (pennies) a quarter represents 25 (pennies) a fifty cent piece (half dollar) represents and a dollar piece represents 100 (pennies). Point out it is much easier to continue then to start all over again. 10 groups of 10 = 100 = 1 dollar Your child has now separated the pennies into 10 piles of 10. Hopefully with the answer yes, then, ask how many dimes add up to a dollar. Help your child arrive to the answer 10. Now, ask your child to place the dimes back into the picture, one above each column. then ask, how many dimes are in the half dollar. Now count the groups of 5. Have your child place a nickel by each 5 pennies. And say with your child "5 pennies is a nickel" for each nickel. So 20 nickels represents 100 pennies which is 1 dollar. Recall the half dollar exercise. How many nickels are in the each of the two groups? 10 nickels is a half dollar. Have your child group the pennies in columns of 10 each. Now count the pennies starting from 1 down one column then the next. When your child reaches 25, group those pennies together, then start counting again, 1 to 25. You should have 4 groups of 25 pennies, 2 and 1/2 columns each. Now have your child place a quarter next to each group. So, each quarter is 25 pennies, and 4 quarters are 100 pennies. Now is a good time to have your child relate dimes and nickels to quarters, quarters to half dollars, etc. Ask your child with all of these choices how he/she would pay for that 1 dollar and 35 cent toy. Explore all possibilities. Finally, have your child play play bank teller. Ask your child to convert one denomination to the other. More advance, ask your child to make change for a purchase of some pretend item. Oh, one final note, its fine to tell your child that $1.35 means 1 dollar and 35 cents. That is, the number to the left of the decimal point (dot) is the number of dollars and the number to the right of the dot is the number of pennies. And don't try to do this all in one sitting! Once another proton is added, it is no longer the same element. The same cannot be applied to when another neutron or another electron is added. Adding more electrons will give the atom a negative charge and removing electrons will give the atom a positive charge. Metals tend to lose electrons, which creates a positive charge. Non-metals tend to gain electrons, forming a negative charge. Electrons are the foundation for determining how compounds are formed amongst atoms. Adding or removing neutrons within an atom changes its isotope. As an example, carbon-12 is the most stable isotope for a carbon atom. However, we can add two more neutrons and carbon-12 is now carbon-14, a less stable isotope of carbon. The number of an isotope directly correlates to the atomic mass of an element. Educational - Cap Back to: Weather Q & A In order for thunderstorms to develop, the air near the surface must be buoyant. Just like air bubbles in water. Since air is less dense then water, any air bubbles released in water float to the surface, they are buoyant. So, think of thunderstorm clouds as being almost like those air bubbles. When the air near the surface becomes buoyant, it bubbles up through the atmosphere. What makes air buoyant? It has to be less dense then the atmosphere surrounding it. Air is less dense than water, so air is buoyant in water. Warm, moist air is less dense then cool air. So, the air near the surface needs to be warm and moist, and the air above needs to be cool and dry. As the air rises, the pressure of the atmosphere surrounding it decreases (there is less atmosphere above pressing down), this causes our warm moist air to cool. So we have another concept to remember: when air goes into an area of lower pressure, it cools. When air is compressed (into an area of higher pressure), it warms. This described by the "Ideal Gas Law". Okay, back to the air rising from the surface. As the buoyant air rises, it cools. This cooling causes the water vapor (gas) in the air to turn to clouds (the water vapor becomes small water droplets). These water droplets are what we see as cloud. That's part one. We understand that thunderstorms form when air near the surface becomes buoyant, rises, cools, and forms clouds. The Gulf Coast area has a VERY special weather situation in Spring and Summer that creates the CAP you are speaking of. Often there is moist air flowing from the Gulf of Mexico over the whole region, and this air is relatively cool, especially if it's already cloudy. At the same time, air higher in the atmosphere is flowing from the West (from the desert) as part of a high pressure system. This dry desert air is being forced to flow downward toward the surface by the high pressure. As the warm air descends, it gets compressed. Remember from above, when air is compressed, it warms further. This forms the "CAP". It's called a cap because it acts just like a bottle cap. The air near the surface is cool and moist, and this dry desert air above is warm. So, the air at the surface ISN'T buoyant. It's cool and dense, while the air above is warm and less dense. So throughout a typical severe thunderstorm day, we start will cool, cloudy air near the surface and warm air aloft. The sun slowly warms the surface air, and erodes the clouds. Our cool, denser surface air is becoming less and less dense as it warms. Eventually, there will be a spot, or several spots over the region where the this moist surface air suddenly becomes warm enough to be buoyant! These spots of buoyant air explode into thunderstorms! Since the air near the surface can only poke through these small holes where the air is buoyant, you tend to get big/severe storms. All of the energy of the warming sun is concentrated into a few places! I think that's really cool. So, next time your area is forecasted to have a cap, look outside from time to time. Watch for the places where the cap breaks first. You'll see thunderstorm clouds suddenly form. I think that's it, thank you so much for coming in. Title: How to Remove Walls in Dwarf Fortress Introduction: In Dwarf Fortress, walls are essential structures that provide protection and define spaces within your fortress. However, there may be situations where you need to remove or destroy existing walls. This could be due to various reasons such as expanding rooms, changing layouts, or fixing mistakes during construction. Removing walls can be challenging if you're new to the game, but with patience and practice, it is definitely achievable. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to remove walls in Dwarf Fortress. Step 1: Identify the Wall You Want to Remove Firstly, navigate through your fortress using the 'z' (for zoom) and arrow keys until you find the wall you wish to demolish. It's crucial to have a clear mental map of what needs to go because once started, the process cannot be undone without reloading an earlier save file. Key Tip: Remember, removing a wall will open up space, potentially exposing areas of your fortress to enemies. Make sure this is what you want before proceeding! Step 2: Designate the Area for Channeling Press 'd' followed by 'b' to access the designation menu. From here, select 'Channel'. Now, carefully highlight the area including the wall(s) you want to remove. Channeling creates a downward slope which effectively removes any obstacle in its path - including walls. Once highlighted, press enter to finalize your selection. Why Channel? Why Not Dig? While digging would seem like the logical choice, dwarves won't dig through constructed structures like walls. Therefore, we must resort to channeling instead. Step 3: Assign Dwarves to the Job To assign dwarves to the task, ensure you're viewing the designated area (press 'k'), then hit 'space'. A job queue window should appear. If no dwarves are assigned yet, simply wait; eventually one or more curious builders might just take up the task. Alternatively, manually assign labors to a dwarf who isn't busy. Go into their profile (accessed via 'u') and enable mining under 'Manual Labor'. Step 4: Monitor Progress & Deal With Debris Keep an eye on the progress bar at the top right corner of the screen. When channeling is complete, debris from the removed wall will remain. To clean up, repeat steps two and three, only this time choosing 'Designate Hauling' ('d', 'h') instead of 'Channel'. Select the pile of rubble left behind and let your diligent dwarves haul it away. Important Note: Be aware that channeling next to flowing water or magma can lead to disastrous floods or fires. Always exercise caution when modifying your fortress' terrain. Conclusion: Remember, careful planning and observation are key when altering your fortress' structure. Take your time understanding these mechanics, experiment wisely, and soon enough, reshaping your subterranean empire will become second nature. Happy fortress-building! Title: How to Get TikTok Emojis TikTok is a popular social media platform that uses emojis as part of its interface and communication system. While many emojis are universally available across devices and platforms, TikTok has its own unique set of emojis that can be used within the app. This tutorial will guide you through obtaining these special TikTok emojis for your personal use. ### Step 1: Understand the Basics To access the TikTok emojis, you need to have the official TikTok application installed on your device (Android or iOS). Make sure you're running the latest version of the app to ensure compatibility with all features. ### Step 2: Create a TikTok Account If you haven't already done so, create a TikTok account by downloading the app from Google Play Store or Apple App Store, then following the sign-up process. Having an account allows you to interact with other users, comment using their exclusive emojis, and even create your content. ### Step 3: Familiarize Yourself With the Interface Spend some time exploring the various sections of the TikTok app such as 'For You', 'Following', 'Discover', etc., to familiarize yourself with where things are located. Pay particular attention to the comment section under videos since this is where most of the action happens regarding using emojis. ### Step 4: Access Available Emojis When writing comments on TikTok, tap on the smiley face icon next to the text input box. A menu will pop up displaying several categories like 'Faves,' 'Reactions,' 'Activities,' among others. Each category contains different sets of emojis specific to TikTok. ### Step 5: Select and Use Emojis Browse through the displayed categories and select any emoji you wish to use. Once selected, it will appear in the comment box ready for posting. Remember, once posted, everyone who views the comment can see the chosen emoji. ### Key Tips & Guidelines: * **Be Respectful**: Always remember to use appropriate language when communicating with others online. Misuse of symbols could lead to misunderstanding or conflict. * **Experiment**: Feel free to try out different combinations of universal and TikTok-specific emojis to express your thoughts creatively. * **Stay Updated**: Regularly update your TikTok app to get new emoji updates and maintain optimal performance. By following these steps and keeping our key tips in mind, you should now be able to enjoy using TikTok's exclusive range of emojis! Happy TikToking! Title: How to Make 2-Ingredient Slime Introduction: Making slime is an exciting and fun activity that you can do at home with just two simple ingredients - white glue and baking soda! This easy-to-follow guide will provide detailed steps, helpful tips, and crucial information to ensure your homemade slime turns out perfect every time. Let's get started! Materials needed: * White PVA (polyvinyl acetate) school glue (1 cup or 8 oz.) * Baking Soda (1 tablespoon) * Food coloring (optional) * Contact lens solution (with boric acid as an ingredient) Step 1: Gather Your Materials Before starting, make sure you have all necessary materials ready. Having everything prepared beforehand ensures a smooth process without any interruptions or distractions. Step 2: Mix Glue and Baking Soda Pour one cup (8 oz.) of white glue into a bowl. Add one tablespoon of baking soda to the same bowl and mix them together thoroughly until they are well combined. The mixture should be smooth and slightly thickened due to the addition of baking soda. Key tip: Stir slowly and carefully so as not to create too many air bubbles within the mixture. Air bubbles may cause inconsistencies during the activation phase later on. Step 3: Activating the Mixture (Adding Contact Lens Solution) Now it's time to activate our slime! To do this, we need to add contact lens solution containing boric acid. Start by adding small amounts (approximately one teaspoon) gradually while stirring continuously. Keep adding more until the consistency starts changing from liquid to solid. Guideline: A good rule of thumb when determining if enough contact lens solution has been added is checking whether the slime pulls away cleanly from the sides of the bowl when lifted. If it does not, continue adding more solution incrementally until this point is reached. Step 4: Kneading the Slime Once the correct amount of contact lens solution has been mixed in, remove the slime from the bowl and begin kneading it with your hands. Be patient; initially, it might stick to your fingers, but keep kneading. After several minutes, it should become less sticky and form a nice, stretchy slime. Key Tip: Dust your hands lightly with baby powder or cornstarch to prevent sticking while kneading. Also, avoid over-kneading which could lead to excessive dryness. Step 5: Customizing Your Slime (Optional) At this stage, feel free to customize your slime according to personal preference. You can add food coloring to change its color, glitter for some sparkle, or even essential oils for fragrance. Simply mix these additional ingredients gently into the slime using a spoon or your fingertips. Step 6: Proper Storage After enjoying playing with your slime, remember to store it properly in an airtight container or resealable plastic bag to maintain its texture and elasticity. It generally lasts up to two weeks if stored correctly. Conclusion: Congratulations! By following these six simple yet detailed steps, you now know how to make your own 2-ingredient slime. Enjoy experimenting with different colors and textures, invite friends over for a DIY slime party, or simply relax and destress while squeezing and stretching your new creation. Happy crafting! Click on the download button next to your preferred package. The full package contains all features while the minimal installation only includes essential components. 3. On November 9, 1989, the SED announced that East Germans would be able to travel to West Berlin the next day. The spokesman who announced the new travel law incorrectly said that it would take effect immediately, implying the Berlin Wall would open that night. People began to gather at border checkpoints at the wall hoping to be let through, but the guards told them that they had no orders to let citizens through. As the number of people grew, the guards became alarmed and tried to contact their superiors but had no responses. Unwilling to use force, the chief guard at the checkpoint relented at 10:54pm and ordered the gate to be opened. Thousands of East-Germans swarmed into West Berlin and the purpose of the wall was deemed now obsolete. The fall of the wall destroyed the SED politically as well as the career of its leader, Egon Krenz. On December 1, 1989, the GDR government revoked the law that guaranteed the SED the right to rule the East German political system, effectively ending communist rule in the GDR. Creating an undeletable and unrenamable folder in Windows can be a useful trick if you want to protect certain files or folders from being accidentally deleted or modified. This tutorial will guide you through the process using the Command Prompt. **Step 1: Open Command Prompt as Administrator** To start, press `Win + X` and select "Command Prompt (Admin)" or "Windows PowerShell (Admin)" from the list that appears. If you see "Windows Terminal" instead, choose that option and then type "cmd" to open the Command Prompt within the terminal window. Running the Command Prompt with administrative privileges is essential because it allows you to make system-wide changes. **Step 2: Navigate to the Desired Directory** Use the `cd` command to navigate to the directory where you wish to create the undeletable and unrenamable folder. For example, if you want to create this special folder inside your user's Documents directory, enter the following commands: ```makefile cd %USERPROFILE%\Documents ``` The `%USERPROFILE%` environment variable points to the current logged-in user's home directory. Replace this part with any other path if needed. **Step 3: Create a New Folder** Next, create a new folder by entering the following command: ```bash mkdir MyInvincibleFolder ``` Replace "MyInvincibleFolder" with the name you prefer for your protected folder. The above command creates a regular folder named "MyInvincibleFolder". We will now proceed to modify its permissions to make it undeletable and unrenamable. **Step 4: Change the Owner of the Folder** By default, newly created directories are owned by the currently logged-in user. To change the ownership of the folder to the built-in "Administrators" group, execute the following commands: ```vbnet takeown /f MyInvincibleFolder /a /r icacls MyInvincibleFolder /setowner "NT SERVICE\TrustedInstaller" /t ``` These two commands first grant ownership to the administrators group recursively, followed by setting the owner explicitly to the Trusted Installer service account - which has high-level access rights over most system components. **Step 5: Modify Permissions** Now, let us restrict all users except the Trusted Installer from modifying our invincible folder. Enter these commands one after another: ```css icacls MyInvincibleFolder /deny Everyone:(D,WD) /t icacls MyInvincibleFolder /grant NT SERVICE\TrustedInstaller:(F) /t ``` The first line denies both delete ("D") and write data ("WD") permissions to everyone including the administrators at the folder level and subdirectories. The second line grants full control permission back only to the Trusted Installer account. At this point, your folder should be considered undeletable and unrenamable, even when attempting to do so while running Command Prompt as an admin. However, there are still ways to remove such restrictions via advanced methods like taking ownership using third-party tools or editing registry keys related to file permissions. Nonetheless, this method provides solid protection against accidental deletion or renaming under normal circumstances. Mommy I'm going to get a different rash on. Dan's Dancing Dog Emergent Literacy Design Rationale: Children need to understand the individual sounds letters make in order to learn to read and write. These sounds are called phonemes. The goal of this lesson is that children will be able to recognize the phoneme /d/. Children will learn to identify the phoneme /d/ and recognize it in everyday words by learning a meaningful symbol and the letter representation. Materials: Primary paper and pencil Chart with "Dan's dancing dogs dared to deliver the dirt" Index cards with the following words: dog, dirt, cat, ball, hand, dare, boat, hat, pond, sad Chart paper and markers Book: Tots on Dots by: Veronica Angel Picture page containing pictures of the following items: pond, bat, dog, desk, cat, door, hand, pencil, book, boat, drum, drink 1.) Introduce the lesson by explaining that we have 26 letters and each of these letters make a particular sound or gesture. We have to move our mouth in certain ways to produce these sounds. 2.) Today we are going to talk about the /d/ sound and the gesture needed to make the sound. Can anyone tell me what letter makes the /d/ sound? Very good, it's the letter d. Some words that have the /d/ sound are dog and dancing. Can anyone else think of words that have the /d/ sound? 3.) I want all of us to look at the sentence I have on this paper. Let's see if we can read this tongue twister. "Dan's dancing dogs dared to deliver the dirt." Very good! Now I want us to read it again and this time separate the /d/ from the rest of the word like this; /d/ an's /d/ ancing .. Let's see if we can do it. 4.) Now let's take out our paper and pencil and practice writing some d's. First I will show you how on the board. First we're going to make a little c and then little d (model slowly how to produce the letter). Now I want you to try. I will come around and see how you're doing. I want each of you to do this 5 times. 5.) Now I want to do an activity. I have picture cards here. I am going to hold up the cards and say the word. If you hear a /d/ I want you to tap your desk 2 times. (Hold up the cards and say the words). 6.) Let's read a book together. This book is called Tots on Dots. After I read the book we will discuss it as a class and then I will read it again slowly. This time when I read it I want you to tap 2 fingers in the palm of your hand whenever you hear the /d/ sound. 7.) For assessment I will pass out a picture page with pictures that do and do not contain the letter d. I will explain to the children what each picture is so that there is no confusion. I will have the children circle the pictures that contain the letter d. I will walk around and monitor the children while they are doing this. Veronica. Tots and Dots. Gainor, Brandi. What's The Point Children begin to understand the value of standard measuring tools when they experiment with different sizes of non-standard units such as paperclips and pencils. Crystals Rule! - Use non-standard units such as paperclips and blocks to measure and compare different sized crystals. This Activity Will Help Your Child - Understand non-standard measurement - Understand comparing the measurements of one item to another - Actual Size by Steve Jenkins - Inch by Inch by Leo Leoni 5 different objects Another object to use as a measurement tool (e.g. paperclip, coin, pencil) How Do I Do It? - Ask your child to pick out three to five objects to measure (e.g., stuffed animal, soda bottle, book). - Choose another object to use as a non-standard measurement tool. The tool should be at least half the size of the smallest object being measured. - On a table, place the objects to be measured. - Ask your child to measure each object with the measurement tool. If you are using coins or paperclips, it may be easier for your child to use a lot of them, lining them up end to end next to the object and then counting them. Ask questions using comparative words such as "taller/shorter," "smaller/bigger," and "wider/thinner." How many pennies long is the book? Is the stuffed animal taller or shorter than the book? Is it wider or thinner? - Try measuring one of the objects with a tool of another length (e.g., a pencil or pen). How many pennies long is the book? How many pencils long is it? - Now show your child the ruler. Explain that the length of the ruler is called a standard: the same length is used by everybody. Help your child measure each object with the ruler. Take It Further Explain to your child that an object can be measured in different ways: length, width, depth. You also can measure only a small part of an object. Let your child use the non-standard tools and the ruler to measure different objects around the house. Show your child how to measure his or her own height. Um and like I said I'm always trying to get him to play with... An' now you're on me till I can't even sit in my chair. An ocean wave is the undulation (rising and falling movement) of the sea surface and is usually caused by winds. Waves are "born" (generated) in the fetch area (where wind and water interact) and travel across the sea until their "death" (collapse) as breakers on some distant shore. You might call this the life cycle of a wave. The wind and the water were and are its parents. The highest part of the wave is called the "crest." The lowest part of the wave is called the "trough." Waves can be described by their: height, wave-length, and wave period. The wave-height is the vertical distance from the crest to the trough. The wavelength is the horizontal distance between the crest of one wave and the crest of the successive (next) wave. The wave period is the time it takes for two successive (one after the other) waves to pass a fixed point. Wave period is used to classify waves. Welcome to the wave family! Title: How to Draw Adorable Kittens: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide ===================================================================== Introduction ------------ Drawing kittens can be an enjoyable and rewarding experience! This comprehensive guide will take you through every step necessary to create adorable kitten drawings with ease and confidence. We'll cover basic shapes, proportions, shading techniques, and more - providing helpful tips along the way. By following this tutorial, even beginners can produce delightful kitten sketches that capture their whimsical charm. So grab your pencils, erasers, and paper; let's get started! ### Supplies Needed 1. Pencil (HB or 2B) 2. Eraser (kneaded or plastic) 3. Paper (smooth sketchbook paper works best) 4. Optional: colored pencils, markers, or watercolors for adding color **Key Tip**: Using a kneaded eraser is ideal as it allows for subtle corrections without leaving smudges behind. Section 1: Creating Basic Shapes ------------------------------ Before diving into specific details like ears, eyes, and noses, we need to establish our kitten's overall form using simple geometric shapes. These foundational elements help ensure proper proportion and balance throughout the drawing process. ### Step 1: Outline the Body *Using light strokes, draw an irregular oval shape for the body.* This initial oval sets the foundation for your kitten's pose and size. It doesn't have to be perfect; slight variations add character and uniqueness to your subject. Remember, real kittens come in various sizes and shapes, so embrace imperfections! ### Step 2: Add Head and Legs *Sketch two smaller overlapping circles for the head and four short lines extending from the bottom of the body for legs.* The circle atop the body represents the head while the four lines signify front and back legs. Make sure they are spaced evenly apart and roughly equal length. At this stage, focus on general placement rather than precise measurements. ![Basic shapes for kitten drawing](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Draw-Kittens-Step-2.jpg/aid695870-v4- Now that's not true of lava flows but when they actually blow I think... Dorothy Stetsko, West Park Elementary, Fresno, CA EROSION Appropriate for grades K-4. OVERVIEW: No matter where you look, the land you see is a battleground. On one side of the battle are the forces beneath the surface. These forces cause the crust to be faulted, folded, tilted, and lifted. On the other side of the battle are the natural processes of weathering and erosion. Once rock has been broken up by weathering the small pieces can be moved by water, ice, wind, or gravity. Everything that happens to cause rocks to be carried away is called erosion. PURPOSE: The following activities will demonstrate to students various types of erosion. The purpose of these activities is to increase students awareness to the point where they can make intelligent decisions on proper land use. OBJECTIVES: Students will be able to: 1. Identify the different types of erosion. 2. Identify the effect of ice on land. 3. Identify the effect of wind on land. 4. Identify the effect if water on landforms. ACTIVITIES: 1. Sand Dunes: How do sand dunes form? Place sand in a pile and blow gently from one side. Observe what happens. What happens when you blow on the sand? Could you make the whole pile move if you blew long enough? 2. Windblown Deposits: Collect the following: newspaper, dry sand in jar with lid, box lid, spoon, water, paper, and pencil. Place the box lid on the center of the paper. Remove the lid from the sand and place it inside the box lid near the center. Blow gently on the sand, increase the strength of your breath until sand is being thrown from the lid. Continue blowing for 5 to 10 seconds at this rate. Examine the material in the paper by rubbing your finger over it. Do the same to the material trapped in the box lid. Which is finer? Why? 3. Water Weight Erosion: How does the weight of water affect the earth? Find a spot of bare dry earth. Pour a cupful of water on it. Repeat on the same spot, but this time hold the cup from as high a distance as possible. Observe, how did the earth change when you poured your first cupful of water? How did it change when you poured the second cupful from a greater height? Can you relate this to changes caused by the weight of water in various places around the earth? 4. Glaciers and Erosion: How does the movement of glaciers cause erosion? Take a 12 inch square piece of aluminum foil and form it into a box shape with edges about 2 inches high. Put it in a freezer overnight. Remove the block. Rub over some clay. What did the block of ice feel like? What happened when you rubbed it over clay? How can you relate this to glaciers? 5. Glacial Erosion: Collect the following materials: ice cube, sand (about 1 spoonful), modeling clay, paper towel, pencil, and paper. Press the ice cube lightly on the flat surface of the modeling clay. Move it back and forth several times. Does anything happen to the clay? To the ice? Place a small pile on the surface of the clay. Place the ice cube over the sand on the clay. Let it sit for about one minute. Pick up the ice cube and look at the surface that had been on the sand. Describe what you see. Place the ice cube back in the same position and move the ice back and forth on the sandy surface of the clay a few times. Remove the ice cube and gently wipe the excess sand off the surface of the clay. Describe the surface of the clay when it was rubbed by the sand and ice. How would this compare with the surface of the land when rock and other materials are dragged over it by a glacier? 6. Landslides: Why do hills and mountains that seem very solid in dry weather develop major landslides after prolonged rains? Build a sand castle. After you have it shaped firmly, pour some water on it. Pour the water slowly and gently. Keep pouring until the sand can absorb no more water. What happened at first? What happened finally? How can you compare this to rainfall and mountains? RESOURCES/MATERIALS NEEDED: All listed above. TYING IT ALL TOGETHER: The natural process of erosion works slowly but surely. In hundreds of thousands of years, erosion can wear away a mountain until it is level with the plain. The more that students know about the causes and preventions of erosion, the more they can do to wisely use the land and not destroy and/or misuse it. One day, Tom and Anna went to the forest with their dad. Dad wanted to chop some wood for the fire. He had a big axe that was very sharp. Tom and Anna liked to watch him swing the axe and make the wood fall. "Can I try the axe, dad?" Tom asked. He wanted to be like his dad and chop wood too. "No, Tom, the axe is too big and heavy for you. You could hurt yourself or someone else. You have to wait until you are older and stronger," dad said. Tom was sad. He wanted to show dad that he could chop wood. He saw a small axe lying on the ground. It was old and rusty, but it looked like it could still cut. Tom picked it up and tried to swing it. "Tom, what are you doing? Put that axe down!" dad shouted. He saw Tom holding the small axe and ran to him. "But dad, I want to chop wood like you. This axe is not big and heavy. It is small and light. See?" Tom said. He swung the axe again, but he did not know how to hold it right. The axe slipped from his hand and flew in the air. Anna saw the axe flying towards her. She was scared. She screamed and ducked. The axe missed her by a hair and landed on a tree. The tree had a hole in it. Out of the hole came a squirrel. The squirrel was holding a shiny thing in its mouth. It was a rare coin. "Wow, look at that!" Anna said. She forgot about the axe and ran to the tree. She wanted to see the shiny thing. "Anna, wait! Don't touch that!" dad said. He followed Anna to the tree. He saw the rare coin in the squirrel's mouth. He knew it was very old and valuable. He wanted to have it. "Hello, little squirrel. Can I have that coin, please?" dad asked. He tried to be nice and friendly. The squirrel looked at dad. It did not know what he was saying. It did not want to give up its shiny thing. It is suitable as a bird home because it is similar to a natural bird home, such as a hollow tree, and birds often make nests inside birdboxes. Birdboxes are often put on trees. They are often made by people who are fond of birds, but they are also useful for studying birds. For domesticated birds, birdboxes are often used for breeding. If a move is made which attacks the opposing king, that king is said to be 'in check'. The player whose king is checked must make a move to remove the check. The options are: moving the king, capturing the threatening piece, or moving another piece between the threatening piece and the king. If the player whose king is in danger cannot do any of these things, it is checkmate, and the player loses the game. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily.
